NYPD Sergeant Promotional Exam — Study Guide

Section 200 — General

Source

  • Patrol Guide Section: 200
  • Source PDF: public-pguide1.pdf
  • Priority: High

Learning Objectives

  • Memorize the NYPD Mission statement and its five components
  • Distinguish between the Mission, Vision, and Values
  • Know the four pledged Values and their exact language
  • Understand how the Mission/Vision/Values apply to supervisory responsibilities

Procedures

Procedure Title Status
P.G. 200-02 Mission, Vision, and Values of the NYPD completed

Chapter Contents

  • section-200-02.md — Full procedure with study notes
  • key-terms.md — Vocabulary and definitions
  • review-questions.md — 5 multiple-choice practice questions

Sergeant Focus Areas

  • Ensuring subordinates understand and follow general departmental policies
  • Exercising supervisory authority consistent with the chain of command
  • Interpreting and applying general orders and directives at the platoon level
  • Accountability for proper dissemination of department-wide directives

P.G. 200-02 — Mission, Vision, and Values of the New York City Police Department

Date Effective: 10/24/25 | Last Revision: R.O. 107 | Page: 1 of 1

Mission

The mission of the New York City Police Department is to enhance the quality of life in New York City by working in partnership with the community to:

  • Enforce the law
  • Preserve peace
  • Protect the people
  • Reduce fear
  • Maintain order

Vision

The NYPD strives to foster a safe and fair City through the strategic deployment of resources, focusing on both:

  • Crime prevention
  • Addressing quality-of-life concerns

While building lasting community relationships.

Values

In partnership with the community, we pledge to:

  1. Protect the lives and property of our fellow citizens and impartially enforce the law
  2. Fight crime, both by preventing it and aggressively pursuing violators of the law
  3. Maintain a higher standard of integrity than is generally expected of others because so much is expected of us
  4. Value human life, respect the dignity of each individual and render our services with courtesy and civility

Sergeant Study Notes

Key Themes to Memorize

Theme Keywords
Mission Enhance quality of life, partnership with community
Vision Safe and fair, strategic deployment, crime prevention, quality-of-life, community relationships
Values Protect, fight crime, integrity, value human life, courtesy, civility

Why This Matters for Sergeants

  • As a supervisor, you are expected to model and enforce these values within your squad
  • The mission statement emphasizes community partnership — expect exam questions framing scenarios where a Sergeant must balance enforcement with community relations
  • The values stress integrity and higher standards — Sergeants are held to an even higher standard as first-line supervisors
  • Impartially enforce the law — a Sergeant must ensure officers apply the law consistently and without bias

Common Exam Angles

  1. Which of the following best describes the NYPD’s mission? — Look for the answer that includes enhance the quality of life and partnership with the community
  2. What are the four pledged values? — Protect, fight crime, integrity, value human life
  3. What does the vision emphasize? — Strategic deployment, crime prevention AND quality-of-life, community relationships
  4. Scenario-based: A Sergeant observes an officer being discourteous — ties back to the value of courtesy and civility

Key Terms — Section 200: General

Term Definition
Mission The core purpose of the NYPD: enhance the quality of life in NYC by working in partnership with the community
Vision The aspirational goal: a safe and fair City through strategic deployment, crime prevention, quality-of-life focus, and community relationships
Values Four pledges: protect lives/property, fight crime, maintain integrity, value human life with courtesy and civility
Quality of Life A recurring theme across the mission and vision — refers to both crime reduction and addressing community concerns
Community Partnership The foundational approach — the NYPD works WITH the community, not just FOR or OVER it
Strategic Deployment Allocating resources based on data and need, central to the NYPD’s vision
Impartially Enforcing the law without bias or favoritism — a core value

Review Questions — Section 200: General

Question 1

According to P.G. 200-02, the mission of the NYPD is to enhance the quality of life in New York City by:

A. Aggressively enforcing all laws and reducing crime statistics B. Working in partnership with the community to enforce the law, preserve peace, protect the people, reduce fear, and maintain order C. Strategically deploying resources to prevent crime and address quality-of-life concerns D. Protecting the lives and property of citizens and impartially enforcing the law

Answer

B. The mission specifically states working in partnership with the community to enforce the law, preserve peace, protect the people, reduce fear, and maintain order. Option C describes the vision, and Option D describes one of the values.

Question 2

Which of the following is NOT one of the four pledged values of the NYPD?

A. Protect the lives and property of our fellow citizens B. Strategically deploy resources to prevent crime C. Maintain a higher standard of integrity than is generally expected of others D. Value human life, respect the dignity of each individual

Answer

B. Strategic deployment of resources is part of the NYPD’s vision, not its values. The four values are: (1) Protect lives/property and impartially enforce the law, (2) Fight crime through prevention and pursuit, (3) Maintain higher integrity standards, (4) Value human life with courtesy and civility.

Question 3

The NYPD’s vision statement focuses on fostering a safe and fair City through:

A. Community policing and partnership B. Strategic deployment of resources, crime prevention, quality-of-life concerns, and community relationships C. Enforcement of the law, preservation of peace, and protection of the people D. Maintaining higher standards of integrity and valuing human life

Answer

B. The vision specifically mentions strategic deployment of resources, focusing on crime prevention AND quality-of-life concerns, while building lasting community relationships. Option A is a general concept, Option C is the mission, and Option D describes the values.

Question 4

A Sergeant observes an officer speaking rudely to a civilian who asked for directions. Which element of P.G. 200-02 is the officer violating?

A. The NYPD mission B. The NYPD vision C. The NYPD values D. None — this is not addressed in P.G. 200-02

Answer

C. The NYPD values pledge to render our services with courtesy and civility. An officer being rude to a civilian directly violates this value.

Question 5

According to P.G. 200-02, why are members of the NYPD expected to maintain a higher standard of integrity?

A. Because they carry firearms B. Because so much is expected of them C. Because they serve the public D. Because of their oath of office

Answer

B. The exact language from the values states: Maintain a higher standard of integrity than is generally expected of others because so much is expected of us.

Score: _____ / 5

Section 202 — Duties & Responsibilities

Source

  • Patrol Guide Section: 202
  • Source PDF: public-pguide1.pdf
  • Priority: High

Learning Objectives

  • Understand the specific duties and responsibilities assigned to each rank within the patrol function
  • Know the Desk Officer’s duties regarding command administration and supervision
  • Identify the Patrol Supervisor’s responsibilities for roll call, patrol, and field supervision
  • Recognize the distinction between duties that are mandatory (shall) versus discretionary (may)

Study Content

File Description
section-202-officers.md Officer-level duties (P.G. 202-01 through 202-17)
section-202-supervisors.md Supervisor-level duties (P.G. 202-18 through 202-39) — MOST CRITICAL for Sergeant exam
key-terms.md Abbreviations, acronyms, roles, and key concepts
review-questions.md 15 multiple-choice practice questions with hidden answers

Procedures

Officer-Level Duties (P.G. 202-01 through 202-17)

Procedure Title Priority Status
P.G. 202-01 Police Officer Medium completed
P.G. 202-02 Radio Motor Patrol Operator Medium completed
P.G. 202-03 Radio Motor Patrol Recorder Medium completed
P.G. 202-04 Motor Scooter Operator Low completed
P.G. 202-05 Neighborhood Safety Team Officer High completed
P.G. 202-06 Administrative Quality of Life Officer Low completed
P.G. 202-08 Youth Coordination Officer Low completed
P.G. 202-09 Arrest Processing Officer Medium completed
P.G. 202-10 Community Affairs Officer Medium completed
P.G. 202-12 Command Auxiliary Police Coordinator Low completed
P.G. 202-13 Evidence/Property Control Specialist Medium completed
P.G. 202-14 Telephone Switchboard Operator Low completed
P.G. 202-15 Police Attendant — Uniformed/Civilian MOS Low completed
P.G. 202-16 Field Intelligence Officer Medium completed
P.G. 202-17 Patrolwagon Operator Low completed

Supervisor-Level Duties (P.G. 202-18 through 202-39)

Procedure Title Priority Status
P.G. 202-18 Desk Officer HIGHEST completed
P.G. 202-19 Patrol Supervisor HIGHEST completed
P.G. 202-20 Supervisor of Neighborhood Safety Team HIGHEST completed
P.G. 202-21 Training Sergeant HIGHEST completed
P.G. 202-25 Field Intelligence Sergeant HIGHEST completed
P.G. 202-26 Lieutenant — Platoon Commander HIGHEST completed
P.G. 202-27 Special Operations Lieutenant High completed
P.G. 202-28 Operations Coordinator Medium completed
P.G. 202-29 Command Integrity Control Officer Medium completed
P.G. 202-30 Executive Officer Medium completed
P.G. 202-31 Supervisor of Patrol (Captain) Medium completed
P.G. 202-32 Duty Captain Low completed
P.G. 202-33 Commanding Officer Medium completed
P.G. 202-34 Duty Inspector Low completed
P.G. 202-35 Duty Chief Low completed
P.G. 202-36 Community Response Team Supervisor HIGHEST completed
P.G. 202-37 Community Response Team Officer Medium completed
P.G. 202-38 Quality of Life Officer Low completed
P.G. 202-39 Quality of Life Sergeant HIGHEST completed
P.G. 202-91 (Cross-reference only) — n/a

Sergeant Focus Areas

  • Mastering all Patrol Supervisor duties including roll call inspections and field supervision
  • Understanding Desk Officer responsibilities and when to assume that role
  • Ensuring proper tour coverage, meal assignments, and post assignments
  • Supervising subordinates’ adherence to their assigned duties and responsibilities
  • NST and CRT supervision requirements (solely assigned sergeant, BWC review, CRAFT forms)
  • Critical numbers: 2-hour AWOL, 5-minute roll call, 5th-of-month profile reports, 4th-hour email, 5-block response, 3-per-vehicle, 5 NST max

Section 202 — Officer-Level Duties & Responsibilities

Source: NYPD Patrol Guide, Section 202 (P.G. 202-01 through 202-17) Exam Relevance: Sergeants must know these duties to supervise, correct, and evaluate subordinates.

P.G. 202-01: Police Officer

Key Duties: - Perform duty in uniform as indicated on roll call - Monitor portable radio at all times while on patrol - Proceed to assigned post/sector as directed by supervisor - Do NOT congregate or engage in unnecessary conversation while on post - Report unusual crime/occurrence to both the patrol supervisor AND the platoon commander

Additional Requirements: - Report conditions not requiring immediate attention to the switchboard operator - Maintain a digital Activity Log throughout the tour - Do NOT leave post/sector until meal actually commences (travel time is NOT authorized)

Sergeant Relevance: A patrol supervisor must verify that officers are on post, properly uniformed, monitoring radios, and maintaining their Activity Logs. The prohibition against congregating is a supervisory enforcement point — the patrol supervisor is specifically tasked with ensuring members do not congregate or engage in unnecessary conversation (see P.G. 202-19). The meal rule (no early departure for travel time) is a common discipline issue.

P.G. 202-02: Radio Motor Patrol (RMP) Operator

Key Duties: - Inspect seatbelts before tour; notify patrol supervisor of any deficiencies - Wear safety belts at all times (may be removed ONLY in dangerous tactical situations) - Inspect Ballistic Window Inserts at start of tour - Permit ONLY uniformed MOS performing related police duty to enter the RMP - Respond to any serious emergency within 5 blocks, even if directed to another assignment

Additional Requirements: - Approach the scene of a reported crime quietly — do NOT use siren unnecessarily - Do NOT carry electric blasting caps in vehicles - Do NOT tow or push vehicles with the RMP (exception: Highway District units equipped with protective bumper guards)

Sergeant Relevance: The patrol supervisor must evaluate driving habits and take corrective action. Seatbelt deficiencies must be reported to the platoon commander; seatbelt vandalism must be reported to the Internal Affairs Bureau. The 5-block rule for serious emergencies is a frequently tested concept. Supervisors should use AVL/DAS to monitor RMP locations.

P.G. 202-03: Radio Motor Patrol (RMP) Recorder

Key Duties: - Ride in the front passenger seat (exception: move to rear when transporting a prisoner or EDP in a vehicle without a partition) - Notify radio dispatcher when any non-MOS enters the RMP (report time, gender, and beginning mileage) - Search the passenger area for contraband after transporting any non-MOS - Carry a duplicate set of keys for the vehicle at all times

Sergeant Relevance: Supervisors should verify that recorders are properly positioned in the front seat and that required notifications are made to the dispatcher when civilians are transported. The post-transport search requirement is a liability and evidence-integrity issue that supervisors must enforce.

P.G. 202-04: Motor Scooter Operator

Key Duties: - Operate motor scooter in accordance with Department guidelines and traffic laws - Wear required protective equipment (helmet, reflective vest) as prescribed - Perform patrol functions similar to foot patrol but with extended range - Report mechanical deficiencies to the patrol supervisor promptly

Sergeant Relevance: Supervisors must ensure operators wear all required safety equipment and that scooters are properly maintained. Equipment deficiency reports flow through the patrol supervisor to the platoon commander.

P.G. 202-05: Neighborhood Safety Team (NST) Officer

Key Duties: - Wear specifically prescribed uniform; must identify as police BEFORE taking enforcement action - Deploy based on crime trends and statistics (data-driven deployment) - No more than 3 NST members (including the supervisor) per vehicle - Must attend NST training before deployment - Do NOT perform duty in plainclothes or conduct decoy operations

Sergeant Relevance: This is a high-priority area for sergeants. NST teams may NOT deploy without a sergeant or above solely assigned to supervision (see P.G. 202-20). The 3-per-vehicle cap and the prohibition on plainclothes/decoy operations are strict rules. Failure to complete required training before deployment is a supervisory accountability issue.

P.G. 202-06: Administrative Quality of Life Officer

Key Duties: - Process quality-of-life summonses and related administrative paperwork - Maintain accurate records of summons activity within the command - Coordinate with the Quality of Life Sergeant on enforcement priorities - Ensure proper documentation and filing of all QOL-related materials

Sergeant Relevance: Supervisors should verify that QOL paperwork is accurate and timely. This role supports the command’s overall quality-of-life enforcement strategy, which is coordinated at the supervisor level by the QOL Sergeant (P.G. 202-39).

P.G. 202-08: Youth Coordination Officer

Key Duties: - Serve as the command’s liaison for juvenile and youth-related matters - Coordinate with schools, youth organizations, and community groups - Maintain awareness of youth crime trends within the command - Assist in juvenile-related investigations and referrals as needed

Sergeant Relevance: Supervisors should ensure the Youth Coordination Officer is properly interfacing with community stakeholders and that juvenile matters are handled in compliance with P.G. 215 (Juvenile Matters). The desk officer should be aware of youth-related incidents reported through this officer.

P.G. 202-09: Arrest Processing Officer

Key Duties: - Process arrests within the command in accordance with Department procedures - Maintain arrest processing area in orderly condition - Ensure all required paperwork, notifications, and system entries are completed - Coordinate with the desk officer regarding prisoner status and processing timelines

Sergeant Relevance: The desk officer directly supervises the arrest processing officer (P.G. 202-18). Supervisors must inspect the arrest processing area and prisoner detention areas. Timeliness and accuracy of arrest processing is a supervisory responsibility.

P.G. 202-10: Community Affairs Officer

Key Duties: - Attend community council meetings as the command representative - Establish and maintain contact with civic and community groups - Maintain liaison with the special operations lieutenant - Administer the MedicAlert and Alzheimer’s Safe Return programs

Sergeant Relevance: Supervisors should ensure the Community Affairs Officer is attending required meetings and maintaining productive community relationships. The liaison with the special operations lieutenant is an important chain-of-communication link. The MedicAlert/Alzheimer’s programs have specific administrative requirements.

P.G. 202-12: Command Auxiliary Police Coordinator

Key Duties: - Coordinate the scheduling, deployment, and supervision of Auxiliary Police within the command - Ensure Auxiliary Police members are properly trained and equipped - Maintain records of Auxiliary Police participation and activity - Report any incidents involving Auxiliary members to the desk officer

Sergeant Relevance: Supervisors must understand the limitations of Auxiliary Police authority and ensure they are not deployed in situations beyond their scope. Any incidents involving Auxiliary members require notification up the chain through the desk officer.

P.G. 202-13: Evidence/Property Control Specialist

Key Duties: - Maintain the command’s property/evidence storage area in secure, organized condition - Process invoiced property in accordance with P.G. 218 (Property — General) - Ensure chain of custody documentation is accurate and complete - Coordinate property pickups and transfers with appropriate units

Sergeant Relevance: The desk officer must inspect the property locker at the commencement of each tour (P.G. 202-18). Supervisors are accountable for the integrity of the evidence/property system. Missing or improperly documented property is a serious supervisory failure.

P.G. 202-14: Telephone Switchboard Operator

Key Duties: - Operate the command telephone switchboard, routing calls appropriately - Record messages and relay information to the desk officer and patrol supervisor - Receive reports of conditions not requiring immediate attention from officers on post - Maintain a log of significant calls and notifications

Sergeant Relevance: The desk officer directly supervises the switchboard operator (P.G. 202-18). The switchboard operator is a key information hub — supervisors should ensure calls are being routed properly and that important messages reach the appropriate personnel without delay.

P.G. 202-15: Police Attendant — Uniformed/Civilian Member of the Service

Key Duties: - Perform designated attendant duties within the command facility - Assist the desk officer with administrative functions as directed - Monitor designated areas of the command facility - Report any irregularities or security concerns to the desk officer

Sergeant Relevance: The desk officer directly supervises the police attendant (P.G. 202-18). Supervisors should ensure attendants are at their assigned positions and performing required duties throughout the tour.

P.G. 202-16: Field Intelligence Officer (FIO)

Key Duties: - Gather, analyze, and disseminate field intelligence relevant to the command - Maintain intelligence files and databases in accordance with Department guidelines - Coordinate with the Field Intelligence Sergeant on intelligence priorities - Prepare intelligence reports and briefings for command staff

Sergeant Relevance: The Field Intelligence Sergeant (P.G. 202-25) directly supervises this officer. Supervisors should ensure intelligence-gathering activities comply with Department guidelines and legal requirements. FIO information feeds into crime-trend analysis used for NST and patrol deployment decisions.

P.G. 202-17: Patrolwagon Operator

Key Duties: - Operate the patrolwagon for prisoner transport and other authorized purposes - Inspect the vehicle at the start of each tour for deficiencies and cleanliness - Ensure the security of prisoners during transport - Coordinate transport requests through the desk officer

Sergeant Relevance: The desk officer directly supervises the patrolwagon operator (P.G. 202-18). Prisoner transport security is a high-liability area. Supervisors must ensure the vehicle is properly inspected and that prisoner handling procedures are followed during every transport.

Quick Reference: Desk Officer’s Direct Reports (Officer Level)

The following officer-level positions report directly to the desk officer (P.G. 202-18):

Position Procedure
Switchboard Operator P.G. 202-14
Police Attendant P.G. 202-15
Command Clerk (administrative)
Patrolwagon Operator P.G. 202-17
Arrest Processing Officer P.G. 202-09
Community Guide (community liaison)

Section 202 — Supervisor-Level Duties & Responsibilities

Source: NYPD Patrol Guide, Section 202 (P.G. 202-18 through 202-39) Exam Relevance: THIS IS THE MOST CRITICAL SECTION for the Sergeant Promotional Exam. Sergeants must master every duty listed for the Desk Officer and Patrol Supervisor roles.

P.G. 202-18: Desk Officer (CRITICAL — 5 pages)

Rank Requirement: Must be a SERGEANT or above. Core Responsibility: Responsible for ALL police operations within the command during the tour.

Before Assuming Duties

  • SHALL inspect the arrest processing area and prisoner detention areas BEFORE assuming desk officer duties
  • SHALL sign name and time in the Command Log when assuming duties
  • SHALL inspect the property locker at the commencement of each tour
  • SHALL query the Medical Division Sick Reporting System at the start of the tour
  • SHALL check Department email at the start of the tour

Personnel Accountability

  • Conduct an immediate investigation when any MOS is unaccounted for at the start of the tour
  • If the member is STILL unaccounted for after 2 hours, comply with A.G. 324-17 (AWOL procedures)
  • This is a mandatory escalation — the 2-hour threshold triggers formal AWOL reporting

Access Control — Behind the Desk

  • Do NOT permit unauthorized persons behind the desk
  • Authorized persons ONLY:
    • Members of the Service (MOS)
    • Judicial officers
    • Medical Examiner (ME)
    • Corporation Counsel
    • Comptroller
    • District Attorney (DA)
    • Non-local law enforcement officers with a judicial warrant

Facility Management

  • SHALL visit ALL areas of the command facility at least ONCE each tour
  • SHALL check restrooms and locker rooms for structural defects permitting visual access (privacy violations)
  • SHALL inspect parking facilities around the command (no illegal parking by MOS)
  • SHALL check Department computer systems frequently
  • SHALL check Department email at start of tour AND at the 4th hour of the tour

Direct Supervision

The desk officer directly supervises the following positions:

Position Procedure
Switchboard Operator P.G. 202-14
Police Attendant P.G. 202-15
Command Clerk (administrative)
Patrolwagon Operator P.G. 202-17
Arrest Processing Officer P.G. 202-09
Community Guide (community liaison)

When Leaving the Desk

  • SHALL sign name and time in the Command Log when leaving desk officer duties
  • Ensure proper relief before departing

Key Exam Points — Desk Officer

  1. The desk officer is responsible for ALL operations within the command during the tour
  2. Property locker inspection happens at the START of every tour
  3. AWOL threshold is 2 hours
  4. Email must be checked at start of tour AND at the 4th hour
  5. Memorize the list of persons authorized behind the desk
  6. Facility walk-through required at least ONCE per tour
  7. Restroom/locker room checks are for structural privacy defects

P.G. 202-19: Patrol Supervisor (CRITICAL)

Rank Requirement: Must be a SERGEANT or above. Core Responsibility: Supervise all police FIELD operations during the tour.

Roll Call

  • SHALL conduct roll call 5 minutes after the start of the tour
  • Verify attendance, uniform compliance, equipment readiness

Field Supervision — Mandatory Responses

  • SHALL immediately respond to:
    • Weapons calls
    • Serious crimes in progress
    • Burglaries
    • Emergencies
  • Response is MANDATORY, not discretionary

Patrol Visits

  • SHALL visit foot and RMP patrol posts FREQUENTLY at IRREGULAR intervals
  • SHALL visit hospital and special posts at least ONCE each tour
  • SHALL ensure members do NOT congregate or engage in unnecessary conversation
  • SHALL use AVL (Automatic Vehicle Locator) in DAS (Domain Awareness System) to monitor RMP locations

Vehicle and Safety Oversight

  • SHALL report serious seatbelt deficiencies to the platoon commander
  • SHALL report seatbelt vandalism to the Internal Affairs Bureau (IAB)
  • SHALL evaluate driving habits of subordinates and take corrective action when needed

Administrative Duties

  • SHALL sign OFFICER PROFILE REPORTS monthly
  • SHALL deliver signed Officer Profile Reports by the 5th of the month

Key Exam Points — Patrol Supervisor

  1. Roll call is 5 minutes after tour start
  2. Must respond to weapons/serious crimes/burglaries/emergencies
  3. Patrol visits must be FREQUENT and IRREGULAR (predictable patterns defeat the purpose)
  4. Hospital/special posts visited at least ONCE per tour
  5. Seatbelt deficiencies go to platoon commander; seatbelt VANDALISM goes to IAB
  6. Officer Profile Reports due by the 5th of each month
  7. AVL/DAS is the tool for monitoring RMP locations remotely

P.G. 202-20: Supervisor of Neighborhood Safety Team (NST)

Rank Requirement: Sergeant or above, SOLELY assigned to NST supervision. Core Rule: NSTs may NOT be deployed without a sergeant or above solely assigned.

Deployment and Supervision

  • SHALL be solely assigned to NST supervision — cannot be split between NST and other duties
  • SHALL supervise no more than 5 NST members per tour
  • SHALL report to the special operations lieutenant
  • SHALL ensure all NST members have completed required NST training before deployment

Accountability Tools

  • SHALL systematically review Body Worn Camera (BWC) video of NST officers
  • SHALL use the CRAFT SUPERVISOR FEEDBACK FORM for commendable actions observed
  • CRAFT form is a positive reinforcement/accountability tool

Key Exam Points — NST Supervisor

  1. NSTs CANNOT deploy without a solely assigned sergeant
  2. Maximum 5 NST members per supervisor per tour
  3. Maximum 3 NST members (including supervisor) per vehicle (from P.G. 202-05)
  4. BWC review is SYSTEMATIC, not random or optional
  5. CRAFT form is for commendable actions

P.G. 202-21: Training Sergeant

Rank Requirement: Sergeant

Key Duties

  • Coordinate and deliver in-service training within the command
  • Ensure all members complete required training on schedule
  • Maintain training records and track compliance
  • Identify training deficiencies and recommend corrective training
  • Coordinate with the Police Academy and borough training units

Sergeant Relevance

The Training Sergeant role is a common assignment for newly promoted sergeants. Accountability for training compliance (firearms requalification, CPR recertification, legal updates) falls on this position.

P.G. 202-25: Field Intelligence Sergeant

Rank Requirement: Sergeant

Key Duties

  • Supervise the Field Intelligence Officer (P.G. 202-16)
  • Coordinate intelligence-gathering activities within the command
  • Ensure intelligence activities comply with Department guidelines and legal standards
  • Brief command staff on intelligence findings and crime trends
  • Coordinate with the Intelligence and Counterterrorism Bureau as needed

Sergeant Relevance

This role requires balancing proactive intelligence gathering with legal and policy constraints. The FI Sergeant ensures that field intelligence feeds into the command’s crime-fighting strategy and CompStat preparations.

P.G. 202-26: Lieutenant — Platoon Commander

Rank Requirement: Lieutenant Core Responsibility: Responsible for ALL command operations during the assigned tour (platoon).

General Duties

  • SHALL cause necessary changes and adjustments in roll call based on conditions
  • SHALL schedule meals so that a supervisor is on duty at ALL times (no gap in supervision)
  • SHALL query the NYPD 360 CCRB Dashboard regularly to monitor complaint trends
  • SHALL respond to radio runs unannounced to observe subordinate performance firsthand

Platoon-Specific Duties

Platoon Special Responsibility
First Platoon (midnight tour) Review reports from the previous 24 hours
Second Platoon (day tour) Responsible for vehicle maintenance and weekly vehicle inspection

Sergeant Relevance

The platoon commander is the sergeant’s immediate supervisor in many commands. Understanding the LT’s responsibilities helps sergeants anticipate what information will be requested and what standards will be enforced. The meal-scheduling rule (always maintain supervisor coverage) directly affects sergeant assignments.

P.G. 202-27: Special Operations Lieutenant

Rank Requirement: Lieutenant

Key Duties

  • Oversee specialized units within the command (NST, anti-crime, conditions teams)
  • Coordinate with the Community Affairs Officer (P.G. 202-10)
  • Manage deployment of specialized resources based on crime data
  • Supervise the NST Supervisor (P.G. 202-20) and related personnel

Sergeant Relevance

NST supervisors and CRT supervisors report through the special operations lieutenant. Understanding this chain of command is essential for sergeants assigned to specialized units.

P.G. 202-28: Operations Coordinator

Rank Requirement: As designated by the commanding officer

Key Duties

  • Coordinate daily operations and resource allocation within the command
  • Prepare and distribute operational orders and directives
  • Maintain operational readiness of command resources
  • Serve as a liaison between the commanding officer and operational units

Sergeant Relevance

The operations coordinator is a key administrative contact for sergeants needing resource allocation, scheduling changes, or operational guidance.

P.G. 202-29: Command Integrity Control Officer (ICO)

Rank Requirement: As designated by the commanding officer

Key Duties

  • Monitor command integrity and compliance with Department policies
  • Conduct random integrity checks and audits
  • Review use-of-force reports, arrest documentation, and other accountability records
  • Report integrity concerns to the commanding officer
  • Coordinate with the Internal Affairs Bureau on integrity matters

Sergeant Relevance

Sergeants should be aware that the ICO monitors compliance within the command. Supervisory lapses (failure to review BWC, inadequate use-of-force documentation, etc.) may be flagged by the ICO. Understanding this role reinforces the importance of thorough supervisory documentation.

P.G. 202-30: Executive Officer (XO)

Rank Requirement: Captain (typically)

Key Duties

  • Serve as second-in-command of the precinct/command
  • Oversee administrative operations and personnel matters
  • Act as commanding officer in the CO’s absence
  • Coordinate with the operations coordinator and platoon commanders
  • Review and approve administrative reports and requests

Sergeant Relevance

The XO is typically the captain who handles day-to-day administrative oversight. Sergeants interact with the XO on personnel issues, scheduling, and administrative approvals.

P.G. 202-31: Supervisor of Patrol (Captain)

Rank Requirement: Captain

Key Duties

  • Overall supervision of patrol operations across multiple platoons
  • Review patrol deployment and staffing levels
  • Evaluate patrol supervisor and platoon commander performance
  • Ensure compliance with patrol standards and directives

P.G. 202-32: Duty Captain

Rank Requirement: Captain

Key Duties

  • Serve as the borough/citywide captain on duty during the assigned tour
  • Respond to major incidents within the borough/area of responsibility
  • Coordinate resources across multiple commands when needed
  • Act as the link between precinct-level supervisors and borough/citywide command

P.G. 202-33: Commanding Officer

Rank Requirement: Captain or above (Inspector for certain commands)

Key Duties

  • Ultimate responsibility for all operations, personnel, and administration within the command
  • Set command priorities and enforcement strategies
  • Conduct CompStat preparation and presentations
  • Approve personnel assignments, transfers, and evaluations
  • Maintain discipline and accountability throughout the command

Sergeant Relevance

The commanding officer sets the tone and priorities that sergeants enforce. Understanding the CO’s responsibilities helps sergeants align their supervision with command objectives.

P.G. 202-34: Duty Inspector

Rank Requirement: Inspector (or Deputy Inspector)

Key Duties

  • Serve as the borough/area inspector on duty
  • Respond to significant incidents requiring inspector-level oversight
  • Coordinate with duty captains and precinct commanding officers

P.G. 202-35: Duty Chief

Rank Requirement: Chief (Assistant Chief or above)

Key Duties

  • Serve as the citywide chief on duty
  • Make command decisions for major incidents requiring chief-level authority
  • Coordinate citywide resources and responses

P.G. 202-36: Community Response Team (CRT) Supervisor (HIGH PRIORITY)

Rank Requirement: Sergeant or above, SOLELY assigned to CRT supervision. Core Rule: CRTs may NOT deploy without a sergeant or above assigned solely to supervision.

Key Duties

  • SHALL attend requisite training BEFORE performing CRT supervisory duties
  • SHALL be solely assigned to CRT supervision during deployment
  • SHALL systematically review Body Worn Camera (BWC) video of CRT officers
  • SHALL use the CRAFT SUPERVISOR FEEDBACK FORM for commendable actions

Key Exam Points — CRT Supervisor

  1. CRTs CANNOT deploy without a solely assigned sergeant (same rule as NST)
  2. Supervisor must complete training BEFORE performing CRT duties
  3. BWC review is systematic (same standard as NST)
  4. CRAFT form used for commendable actions (same tool as NST)
  5. The NST and CRT supervisor requirements are nearly identical — exam may test whether you can distinguish them or recognize the parallel structure

P.G. 202-37: Community Response Team Officer

Key Duties

  • Perform community response duties as directed by the CRT supervisor
  • Engage with community members in designated areas
  • Address quality-of-life conditions and community concerns
  • Maintain required documentation of community interactions

Sergeant Relevance

CRT officers operate under direct sergeant supervision. The supervisor is accountable for their deployment, conduct, and documentation.

P.G. 202-38: Quality of Life Officer

Key Duties

  • Address quality-of-life conditions within the command
  • Issue summonses for quality-of-life violations
  • Coordinate with the Quality of Life Sergeant on enforcement priorities
  • Document conditions and enforcement actions taken

P.G. 202-39: Quality of Life Sergeant (HIGH PRIORITY)

Rank Requirement: Sergeant

Key Duties

  • Supervise Quality of Life Officers (P.G. 202-38) and Administrative QOL Officers (P.G. 202-06)
  • Set quality-of-life enforcement priorities based on community complaints and command direction
  • Coordinate QOL enforcement strategy with the platoon commander and special operations lieutenant
  • Review and approve QOL-related documentation and summons activity
  • Ensure QOL enforcement is consistent, fair, and aligned with community needs

Sergeant Relevance

This is a direct sergeant assignment. The QOL Sergeant balances enforcement with community relations and must coordinate across multiple officer-level positions.

P.G. 202-91: Cross-Reference

This is NOT a standalone procedure. It serves as an internal cross-reference within the Patrol Guide.

Supervisor Roles — Comparison Matrix

Feature Desk Officer (202-18) Patrol Supervisor (202-19) NST Supervisor (202-20) CRT Supervisor (202-36)
Minimum Rank Sergeant Sergeant Sergeant Sergeant
Scope ALL command operations ALL field operations NST unit only CRT unit only
Location Inside command facility In the field In the field In the field
Solely Assigned Yes (at desk) Yes (to field) YES (mandatory) YES (mandatory)
BWC Review N/A As needed Systematic Systematic
CRAFT Form N/A N/A Yes Yes
Max Subordinates N/A (facility staff) N/A (all field MOS) 5 per tour Per assignment
Reports To Platoon Commander Platoon Commander Special Ops Lt Special Ops Lt

Critical Numbers for the Exam

Number Context Procedure
2 hours AWOL threshold — trigger formal reporting 202-18
4th hour Second mandatory email check 202-18
5 minutes Roll call starts after tour begins 202-19
5th of month Officer Profile Reports due 202-19
5 members Maximum NST officers per supervisor per tour 202-20
3 per vehicle Maximum NST members (including supervisor) per vehicle 202-05
5 blocks RMP must respond to serious emergency within this range 202-02
Once per tour Minimum facility walk-through (Desk Officer) 202-18
Once per tour Minimum hospital/special post visit (Patrol Supervisor) 202-19

Section 202 — Key Terms, Abbreviations & Acronyms

Source: NYPD Patrol Guide, Section 202

Abbreviations & Acronyms

Abbreviation Full Term Context
RMP Radio Motor Patrol Standard patrol vehicle; operator and recorder roles defined in P.G. 202-02 and 202-03
AVL Automatic Vehicle Locator GPS-based system for tracking RMP locations; used by patrol supervisors via DAS (P.G. 202-19)
DAS Domain Awareness System NYPD technology platform integrating surveillance, sensor data, and AVL; patrol supervisors use AVL within DAS
BWC Body Worn Camera Required equipment; NST and CRT supervisors must systematically review BWC video (P.G. 202-20, 202-36)
CRAFT (Supervisor Feedback Form) CRAFT SUPERVISOR FEEDBACK FORM used by NST and CRT supervisors to document commendable actions
CCRB Civilian Complaint Review Board Independent oversight agency; platoon commanders must query the NYPD 360 CCRB Dashboard regularly (P.G. 202-26)
NST Neighborhood Safety Team Specialized patrol unit deployed based on crime data; strict supervision requirements (P.G. 202-05, 202-20)
CRT Community Response Team Community-focused patrol unit; supervision requirements parallel NST (P.G. 202-36, 202-37)
ECMS Evidence Collection and Management System System for tracking invoiced property and evidence; relates to Evidence/Property Control Specialist (P.G. 202-13)
EDP Emotionally Disturbed Person Relevant to RMP recorder seating rules — recorder moves to rear seat when transporting EDP in vehicle without partition (P.G. 202-03)
FORMS Field Online Reporting and Management System Digital system for maintaining reports and activity logs
CPR System Complaint, Personnel, and Report System Department system for tracking complaints, personnel records, and reports
IAB Internal Affairs Bureau Receives reports of seatbelt vandalism from patrol supervisors (P.G. 202-19)
ICO Integrity Control Officer Command-level position monitoring policy compliance and integrity (P.G. 202-29)
MOS Member of the Service Any sworn or civilian employee of the NYPD
QOL Quality of Life Refers to quality-of-life enforcement, officers (P.G. 202-38), and sergeants (P.G. 202-39)
XO Executive Officer Second-in-command of a precinct/command (P.G. 202-30)
CO Commanding Officer Head of a precinct/command (P.G. 202-33)
FIO Field Intelligence Officer Gathers and analyzes field intelligence; supervised by Field Intelligence Sergeant (P.G. 202-16)
A.G. Administrative Guide Internal NYPD administrative procedures; A.G. 324-17 governs AWOL reporting

Key Roles

Role Procedure Minimum Rank Key Responsibility
Desk Officer P.G. 202-18 Sergeant ALL command operations during tour; facility management; direct supervision of station house personnel
Patrol Supervisor P.G. 202-19 Sergeant ALL field operations during tour; roll call; patrol visits; responding to serious incidents
Platoon Commander P.G. 202-26 Lieutenant ALL command operations during assigned platoon; roll call adjustments; meal scheduling; vehicle maintenance (2nd platoon)
Special Operations Lieutenant P.G. 202-27 Lieutenant Oversees specialized units (NST, CRT, conditions teams); receives reports from NST/CRT supervisors
Integrity Control Officer (ICO) P.G. 202-29 As designated Monitors command integrity and policy compliance; conducts audits
Operations Coordinator P.G. 202-28 As designated Coordinates daily operations and resource allocation
NST Supervisor P.G. 202-20 Sergeant Solely assigned supervision of up to 5 NST members; BWC review; CRAFT forms
CRT Supervisor P.G. 202-36 Sergeant Solely assigned supervision of CRT; BWC review; CRAFT forms
QOL Sergeant P.G. 202-39 Sergeant Supervises QOL enforcement strategy and personnel
Training Sergeant P.G. 202-21 Sergeant Coordinates in-service training; tracks compliance
Field Intelligence Sergeant P.G. 202-25 Sergeant Supervises FIO; coordinates intelligence activities

Key Concepts

Command Log

  • Official record maintained at the desk
  • Desk officer signs name and time when assuming AND leaving duties (P.G. 202-18)
  • Permanent record of command operations

Activity Log (Digital)

  • Maintained by each police officer during the tour (P.G. 202-01)
  • Records patrol activity, observations, and actions taken
  • Digital format maintained through Department systems

Roll Call

  • Conducted by the patrol supervisor 5 minutes after start of tour (P.G. 202-19)
  • Verifies attendance, uniform compliance, and equipment readiness
  • Platoon commander may cause necessary changes/adjustments (P.G. 202-26)

Interrupted Patrol Log

  • Record of interruptions to normal patrol activity
  • Documents reasons for leaving assigned post/sector
  • Supports accountability for time spent off normal patrol

Officer Profile Report

  • Monthly supervisory document signed by the patrol supervisor
  • Must be delivered by the 5th of each month (P.G. 202-19)
  • Documents officer performance and supervisory observations

CRAFT Supervisor Feedback Form

  • Used by NST supervisors (P.G. 202-20) and CRT supervisors (P.G. 202-36)
  • Documents commendable actions by subordinates
  • Positive accountability tool — reinforces good performance

NYPD 360 CCRB Dashboard

  • Queried regularly by the platoon commander (P.G. 202-26)
  • Tracks civilian complaint trends within the command
  • Used for early identification of complaint patterns

Shall vs. May — Mandatory vs. Discretionary

Understanding the distinction is critical for the Sergeant exam:

Term Meaning Example
Shall Mandatory — MUST be done The patrol supervisor SHALL conduct roll call 5 minutes after the start of tour
Must Mandatory — same force as shall Must be a sergeant or above
Ensure Supervisory mandate — the supervisor is accountable for the outcome Ensure members do not congregate
May Discretionary — permitted but not required Seatbelts may be removed ONLY in dangerous tactical situations
Should Recommended — expected but with some flexibility Less common in Section 202

Exam Tip: When an answer choice includes shall or must language from the Patrol Guide, it is almost always the correct answer over choices using softer language.

Section 202 — Review Questions

Source: NYPD Patrol Guide, Section 202 Focus: Sergeant-relevant duties and responsibilities Format: Multiple-choice with hidden answers

Question 1

The desk officer is required to check Department email at which of the following times during the tour?

A. At the start of the tour only B. At the start of the tour and at the 2nd hour C. At the start of the tour and at the 4th hour D. Every 2 hours throughout the tour

Answer C. Per P.G. 202-18, the desk officer shall check Department email at the start of the tour AND at the 4th hour of the tour. These are the two mandatory email check times.

Question 2

A member of the service is unaccounted for at the start of the tour. The desk officer conducts an immediate investigation but the member remains unaccounted for. At what point must the desk officer initiate AWOL procedures in accordance with A.G. 324-17?

A. After 1 hour B. After 2 hours C. After 3 hours D. After 4 hours

Answer B. Per P.G. 202-18, if a member is still unaccounted for after 2 hours, the desk officer must comply with A.G. 324-17 (AWOL procedures). The desk officer should conduct an immediate investigation first, but the 2-hour mark triggers the formal AWOL process.

Question 3

Which of the following persons is NOT authorized to be behind the desk at a precinct station house?

A. A District Attorney B. A representative from the Comptroller’s office C. A local community council president D. A non-local law enforcement officer with a judicial warrant

Answer C. Per P.G. 202-18, only the following are authorized behind the desk: MOS, judicial officers, Medical Examiner, Corporation Counsel, Comptroller, District Attorney, and non-local law enforcement officers with a judicial warrant. A community council president, regardless of their relationship with the command, is NOT authorized behind the desk.

Question 4

The patrol supervisor shall conduct roll call at what time relative to the start of the tour?

A. At the exact start of the tour B. 5 minutes after the start of the tour C. 10 minutes after the start of the tour D. 15 minutes after the start of the tour

Answer B. Per P.G. 202-19, the patrol supervisor shall conduct roll call 5 minutes after the start of the tour. This allows time for members to assemble and prepare.

Question 5

A patrol supervisor discovers that a seatbelt in an RMP has been intentionally vandalized. To whom must this be reported?

A. The platoon commander B. The desk officer C. The Internal Affairs Bureau D. The operations coordinator

Answer C. Per P.G. 202-19, seatbelt vandalism must be reported to the Internal Affairs Bureau (IAB). This is distinct from seatbelt deficiencies (mechanical failures or wear), which are reported to the platoon commander. Vandalism implies intentional damage, which is an integrity issue warranting IAB involvement.

Question 6

The patrol supervisor is required to sign Officer Profile Reports monthly and deliver them by what date?

A. The 1st of the month B. The 5th of the month C. The 10th of the month D. The 15th of the month

Answer B. Per P.G. 202-19, the patrol supervisor shall sign Officer Profile Reports monthly and deliver them by the 5th of the month.

Question 7

Which of the following best describes how the patrol supervisor should visit foot and RMP patrol posts?

A. At scheduled intervals announced during roll call B. Once at the beginning and once at the end of the tour C. Frequently at irregular intervals D. Only when responding to a radio run in the area

Answer C. Per P.G. 202-19, the patrol supervisor shall visit foot and RMP patrol posts FREQUENTLY at IRREGULAR intervals. The key words are frequently and irregular — predictable patterns of supervision defeat the purpose and allow officers to anticipate when the supervisor will check.

Question 8

Neighborhood Safety Teams (NSTs) may NOT be deployed without which of the following?

A. Approval from the borough commander B. A lieutenant solely assigned to NST supervision C. A sergeant or above solely assigned to NST supervision D. At least 6 NST members available for the tour

Answer C. Per P.G. 202-20, NSTs may NOT be deployed without a sergeant or above solely assigned to NST supervision. The key word is solely — the supervisor cannot be splitting duties between NST and other assignments. The minimum rank is sergeant, not lieutenant.

Question 9

What is the maximum number of NST members a single NST supervisor may supervise per tour?

A. 3 B. 4 C. 5 D. 8

Answer C. Per P.G. 202-20, the NST supervisor shall supervise no more than 5 NST members per tour. Note that this is a separate limit from the 3-per-vehicle rule (P.G. 202-05), which limits the number of NST members including the supervisor in any single vehicle.

Question 10

An RMP operator observes a serious emergency 4 blocks from their current location. The operator has been directed by the dispatcher to respond to a different assignment. What should the operator do?

A. Continue to the assigned call as directed by the dispatcher B. Respond to the serious emergency since it is within 5 blocks C. Radio the patrol supervisor for guidance before taking action D. Respond only if no other units are available

Answer B. Per P.G. 202-02, the RMP operator shall respond to a serious emergency within 5 blocks, even if directed to another assignment. The 5-block rule for serious emergencies takes priority over the dispatcher’s assignment. At 4 blocks, the emergency is within the mandatory response range.

Question 11

The desk officer is required to inspect which of the following BEFORE assuming desk officer duties?

A. The property locker and parking facilities B. The arrest processing area and prisoner detention areas C. All restrooms and locker rooms D. The command’s vehicle fleet

Answer B. Per P.G. 202-18, the desk officer shall inspect the arrest processing area and prisoner detention areas BEFORE assuming duties. The property locker is inspected at the commencement of each tour (after assuming duties). Restrooms/locker rooms and parking facilities are checked during the tour but are not pre-assumption requirements.

Question 12

Regarding RMP seatbelts, under what circumstance may a member of the service remove their safety belt?

A. When conducting a vehicle stop B. When approaching a scene of a reported crime C. Only in dangerous tactical situations D. When transporting a prisoner

Answer C. Per P.G. 202-02, safety belts shall be worn at all times and may be removed ONLY in dangerous tactical situations. None of the other listed activities — vehicle stops, approaching crime scenes, or transporting prisoners — authorize removal of the safety belt.

Question 13

The CRT Supervisor shares several requirements in common with the NST Supervisor. Which of the following is required of the CRT Supervisor that is NOT explicitly required of the NST Supervisor?

A. Systematic BWC video review B. Use of the CRAFT Supervisor Feedback Form C. Completing requisite training BEFORE performing CRT supervisory duties D. Being solely assigned to supervision during deployment

Answer C. Per P.G. 202-36, the CRT supervisor must attend requisite training BEFORE performing CRT supervisory duties. While both roles require BWC review, CRAFT forms, and sole assignment, the explicit pre-duty training requirement is specifically highlighted for the CRT supervisor. Both roles are functionally similar, but the exam may test this distinction.

Question 14

Which platoon commander is specifically responsible for vehicle maintenance and weekly vehicle inspection?

A. First Platoon (midnight tour) B. Second Platoon (day tour) C. Third Platoon (evening tour) D. All platoon commanders share this duty equally

Answer B. Per P.G. 202-26, the Second Platoon (day tour) lieutenant is responsible for vehicle maintenance and weekly vehicle inspection. The First Platoon (midnight tour) lieutenant is responsible for reviewing reports from the previous 24 hours.

Question 15

A police officer assigned to a Neighborhood Safety Team is asked to conduct a decoy operation targeting robbery suspects. Which of the following is correct?

A. The officer may conduct the operation with NST supervisor approval B. The officer may conduct the operation if wearing the prescribed NST uniform C. The officer must NOT conduct decoy operations as an NST member D. The officer may conduct the operation only during the first platoon

Answer C. Per P.G. 202-05, NST officers must NOT perform duty in plainclothes or conduct decoy operations. This is an absolute prohibition regardless of supervisory approval or uniform status. Decoy operations are outside the scope of NST duties.

Section 207 — Complaints

Source

  • Patrol Guide Section: 207
  • Source PDF: public-pguide1.pdf
  • Priority: High

Learning Objectives

  • Understand the procedures for receiving and processing complaints from the public
  • Know the classification of complaints and the proper documentation required
  • Identify the Patrol Supervisor’s role in responding to and investigating complaints
  • Recognize procedures for crime scene management and evidence preservation
  • Master the notification matrix for specialized units and external agencies
  • Understand bias incident response, classification, and reporting timelines
  • Know corruption reporting obligations and the civilian complaint process
  • Understand missing person procedures and alert system activation criteria

Study Guide Files

File Topics Covered
section-207-complaint-system.md Complaint reporting framework: where complaints are recorded, continuous events, voiding vs. unfounded, complaint file (207-01, 02, 03, 04, 05, 09, 34)
section-207-investigations.md Preliminary investigations, detective/unit referral thresholds, bias incidents, sex crimes, carjackings (207-07, 08, 10, 29, 33)
section-207-corruption-civilian.md Corruption reporting duty, IAB procedures, civilian complaint processing (207-21, 22, 27, 28)
section-207-missing-alerts.md Missing persons, Silver Alert, Blue Alert systems (207-23, 31, 39)
section-207-notifications.md Notification matrix: Operations Unit, IAB, specialized units (207-06, 24)
key-terms.md All key terms, forms, and abbreviations from Section 207
review-questions.md 20 multiple-choice practice questions with hidden answers

Procedures

Procedure Title Status
P.G. 207-01 Complaint Reporting System completed
P.G. 207-02 Complaints Not Recorded on Complaint Report completed
P.G. 207-03 Voiding Complaint Reports completed
P.G. 207-04 Reporting of Complaints Occurring in Another Command completed
P.G. 207-05 Duplicate Copies of Complaint Reports completed
P.G. 207-06 Notifications completed
P.G. 207-07 Preliminary Investigation of Complaints (Other Than Vice/Narcotics) completed
P.G. 207-08 Preliminary Investigation of Vice Related, Narcotics, or Organized Crime Complaints completed
P.G. 207-09 Follow-Up Investigations of Complaints Already Recorded completed
P.G. 207-10 Bias Motivated Incidents completed
P.G. 207-11 Preliminary Investigation, Recording and Transmission of Alarms for Stolen Vehicles completed
P.G. 207-12 Lost or Stolen Property/Identity Theft completed
P.G. 207-13 Lost/Stolen Vehicle Plates, Licenses and Other DMV Documents completed
P.G. 207-16 Overdue Rental Vehicles completed
P.G. 207-17 Contraband Weapons completed
P.G. 207-18 Complaints Involving Credit Cards completed
P.G. 207-20 Investigation of Crimes Involving Specific Topics completed
P.G. 207-21 Allegations of Corruption and Other Misconduct Against Members of the Service completed
P.G. 207-22 Allegations of Corruption Against City Employees completed
P.G. 207-23 Missing Persons completed
P.G. 207-24 Notifications from Caseworkers (Adult Protective Services/Dept for the Aging) completed
P.G. 207-25 Voluntary Surrender of Weapons completed
P.G. 207-26 Field Reports completed
P.G. 207-27 Civilian Complaints - Witness Statement completed
P.G. 207-28 Processing Civilian Complaints completed
P.G. 207-29 Investigation of Carjackings completed
P.G. 207-30 Complaints Involving Identity Theft completed
P.G. 207-31 Silver Alert System completed
P.G. 207-32 Operational Guidelines for Processing completed
P.G. 207-33 Complaints Involving Sex Crimes completed
P.G. 207-34 Complaint File completed
P.G. 207-35 Communications Received from the Mayor’s Office completed
P.G. 207-36 Grievance Procedures for Members of the Public with Disabilities completed
P.G. 207-37 Review of Requests for U Visa Certifications completed
P.G. 207-38 Available Transportation Services for Complainants, Victims and Witnesses Who Are Disabled completed
P.G. 207-39 Blue Alert System completed

Sergeant Focus Areas

  • Complaint Recording Jurisdiction: Mastering where complaints are recorded (precinct of occurrence rules, continuous event exceptions for homicide and Rape 1st Degree, Transit/Housing rules)
  • Investigation Thresholds: Dollar amounts triggering Major Case Unit ($100K) and detective squad referrals ($5K/$10K Manhattan), victim age (60+), and repeat victimization (6 months)
  • Bias Incident Response: The full escalation chain, the CO’s duty to personally interview victims, 10-day reporting deadline, and that only the Hate Crime Unit CO determines bias classification
  • Corruption Reporting: Absolute duty to report, Confidential ID Number process, failure to report as misconduct, and the interview sequence when a member is accused
  • Civilian Complaints: Investigating Supervisor hierarchy (Platoon Commander first), reviewer rank requirement (one rank higher), and immediate Command Log entry for blank form requests
  • Missing Persons: No minimum time limits, resident precinct recording, anyone can be a complainant, never refer to Missing Persons Squad directly
  • Alert Systems: Silver Alert activation (Captain+ from Detective Bureau, 24-hour window, under-65 exception), Blue Alert criteria
  • Notification Matrix: Which situations require Operations Unit notification, which go direct, and the IAB notification pattern (DOH, tax, postal, police impersonation)
  • Sex Crimes: SVU notification for all sex crimes, victim’s right to advocate, hospital walk-ins go directly to SVU without patrol response

Section 207 — Complaint Reporting System

Covers P.G. 207-01, 207-02, 207-03, 207-04, 207-05, 207-09, 207-34

P.G. 207-01: Complaint Reporting System

Key Definitions

Term Definition
Complaint A report of an alleged criminal offense or other matter requiring police attention
Pick-Up Arrest An arrest of a person wanted by another command or agency
Continuous Event A crime or series of related crimes spanning multiple locations or jurisdictions
Incident A single event or occurrence reported in a complaint
Offense A specific criminal charge; up to 10 offenses may be recorded per incident

Where Complaints Are Recorded

The general rule: complaints are recorded in the PRECINCT OF OCCURRENCE.

Exceptions to Precinct of Occurrence

Situation Recorded At
Vice/Narcotics/Organized Crime Specialty Enforcement Division
Vice exception: pick-up arrests Precinct of arrest
Vice exception: found drugs IN building Precinct of occurrence
Vice exception: found drugs 2 oz or less NOT in building Precinct of occurrence
Missing NYC residents Missing person’s resident precinct
Piers/bulkheads/bodies in navigable waters Precinct where pier or bulkhead is located
Lost property Precinct where complainant FIRST discovered the loss
Overdue rental vehicles Precinct where vehicle was leased/rented OR where it was expected to be returned

Continuous Event Rules

  • General rule: Record at the precinct of the INITIAL offense.
  • Homicide exception: Recorded where the homicide occurred, regardless of where the continuous event began.
  • Rape 1st Degree exception: Recorded where the rape occurred, regardless of where the continuous event began.

Special Bureau Jurisdiction

Bureau Rule
Housing Bureau Continuous event starting on-development remains Housing Bureau jurisdiction
Transit Bureau Crime on a moving train is recorded at the NEXT station where the train stops

Forms

Form Number Purpose
Complaint Report Worksheet PD313-152A Initial recording of complaint information
Complaint Report PD313-152 Official complaint record
Complaint Index Report — Indexing and cross-referencing
Omniform Complaint Revision — Amending/revising existing complaint reports

P.G. 207-02: Complaints Not Recorded on Complaint Report

Certain matters brought to the attention of the Department do not warrant a formal Complaint Report. These are handled through alternative documentation methods (e.g., Command Log entries, aided reports, or other administrative records) as appropriate to the nature of the complaint.

Sergeant Focus: Ensure members understand which incidents require a formal Complaint Report and which are properly documented through alternative means. Misdocumentation can affect crime statistics and case tracking.

P.G. 207-03: Voiding Complaint Reports

When a Report May Be Voided

The Desk Officer determines if a complaint should be voided when it is: - A duplicate of an existing report - Filed at the wrong precinct - For an event occurring outside NYC - An event that should have been recorded differently

Critical Distinction: VOIDED vs. UNFOUNDED

Status Meaning
VOIDED The report should NOT have been prepared in the first place (procedural error)
UNFOUNDED The report was properly prepared, but subsequent investigation reveals that no offense actually occurred

Exam Alert: This distinction is heavily tested. A voided report is a clerical/procedural correction. An unfounded case reflects an investigative finding that no crime took place.

P.G. 207-04: Reporting of Complaints Occurring in Another Command

When a complaint is reported at a precinct other than the precinct of occurrence: - The receiving command documents the complaint and ensures the information is transmitted to the precinct of occurrence for proper recording. - The precinct of occurrence retains responsibility for the complaint and any subsequent investigation.

Sergeant Focus: Supervisors must ensure that complaints taken for another command are promptly and accurately forwarded. Delays can compromise investigations.

P.G. 207-05: Duplicate Copies of Complaint Reports

Procedures govern when and how duplicate copies of complaint reports are prepared and distributed. Duplicate copies ensure that all relevant commands, squads, and units have the information necessary to conduct follow-up investigations or provide support.

P.G. 207-09: Follow-Up Investigations of Complaints Already Recorded

When additional information is obtained on a previously recorded complaint: - The follow-up investigation is coordinated with the detective squad or specialized unit assigned to the case. - Updates are documented through the Omniform Complaint Revision process. - The original complaint report’s status (active, closed, unfounded) is updated accordingly.

Sergeant Focus: Patrol supervisors should ensure that officers conducting follow-up canvasses or receiving new information properly route it to the assigned investigative unit rather than creating new complaint reports.

P.G. 207-34: Complaint File

The Complaint File is the centralized repository for all complaint-related documentation within a command. It includes: - Original complaint reports and worksheets - Follow-up investigation notes - Correspondence related to cases - Status updates and dispositions

Sergeant Focus: Supervisors are responsible for ensuring the complaint file is maintained in an orderly and up-to-date fashion, facilitating case review and audits.

Supervisor Quick Reference

Complaint Recording Decision Tree

Is it a criminal offense or police matter?
├── NO → Document via Command Log or other appropriate record (207-02)
└── YES → Prepare Complaint Report
    ├── Is it Vice/Narcotics/Organized Crime?
    │   ├── YES → Specialty Enforcement Division (with exceptions)
    │   └── NO → Continue
    ├── Is it a missing NYC resident?
    │   ├── YES → Resident precinct
    │   └── NO → Continue
    ├── Is it a continuous event?
    │   ├── YES → Precinct of INITIAL offense
    │   │   ├── UNLESS homicide → where homicide occurred
    │   │   └── UNLESS Rape 1st → where rape occurred
    │   └── NO → Precinct of OCCURRENCE
    └── Lost property? → Where loss FIRST discovered

Section 207 — Corruption Allegations and Civilian Complaints

Covers P.G. 207-21, 207-22, 207-27, 207-28

P.G. 207-21: Allegations of Corruption and Other Misconduct Against Members of the Service

Critical procedure — tested heavily on the Sergeant exam.

Absolute Duty to Report

ALL members of the service have an ABSOLUTE DUTY to report corruption and misconduct.

This is not discretionary. Every member who becomes aware of corruption or other misconduct must report it.

How to Report

  • Report to the IAB Command Center (operates 24 hours)
  • Reports can be made anonymously
  • When reporting anonymously, the member receives a Confidential ID Number

Confidential ID Number

  • Obtaining a Confidential ID Number satisfies the reporting responsibility
  • The member need not identify themselves — the Confidential ID Number serves as proof that the report was made

Failure to Report

  • FAILURE TO REPORT corruption or misconduct is itself serious misconduct
  • Conduct undertaken to cover up corruption constitutes obstruction of justice

Exam Alert: Three critical points: (1) Absolute duty — no exceptions, (2) Confidential ID Number satisfies the duty, (3) Failure to report is itself misconduct.

When an Allegation Is Made Against You

If a member of the service has an allegation made against them, the procedure is:

  1. The accused member requests a supervisor to respond
  2. The supervisor interviews the complainant
  3. The supervisor confers with IAB
  4. BEFORE interviewing the accused member

Exam Alert: The sequence matters. The supervisor interviews the complainant and contacts IAB BEFORE interviewing the member accused of misconduct.

P.G. 207-22: Allegations of Corruption Against City Employees

When allegations of corruption are made against city employees (non-NYPD): - The receiving member documents the allegation - The information is routed to IAB and the appropriate Inspector General for the relevant city agency - Procedures parallel those for members of the service, but the investigative authority may differ depending on the agency involved

P.G. 207-27: Civilian Complaints — Witness Statement

When a civilian complaint is received, witness statements are a critical component of the investigation: - Statements from witnesses are obtained to corroborate or refute the allegation - The investigating supervisor ensures that all available witnesses are interviewed - Witness statements are documented and included in the complaint file

P.G. 207-28: Processing Civilian Complaints

7 pages in the Patrol Guide — critical for supervisors.

Investigating Supervisor

The Investigating Supervisor is defined by a specific hierarchy:

Priority Title
1st Platoon Commander
2nd Special Operations Lieutenant
3rd Integrity Control Officer (ICO)
If all unavailable CO or Duty Captain

Reviewer Rank Requirement

The Supervisor Reviewing a Civilian Complaint must be at least ONE RANK HIGHER than the member who is the subject of the complaint.

Where Complaints Can Be Made

Civilian complaints can be made at ANY of the following locations: - Patrol precinct - Police Service Area (PSA) - Transit district - Traffic unit - Internal Affairs Bureau (IAB) - Any Department office

Methods of Filing a Complaint

Method Notes
In person Complainant appears and wishes to file immediately
By mail Written complaint received through postal mail
By telephone Complaint received via phone call
In person, not wanting to file immediately Complainant appears but is not ready to file at that time

Request for Blank Complaint Form

When someone requests a blank civilian complaint form: 1. The Desk Officer is advised 2. A Command Log entry is made IMMEDIATELY

Exam Alert: The immediate Command Log entry when someone merely requests a blank form is a testable detail. It applies even if the person does not ultimately file a complaint.

Supervisor Quick Reference — Corruption and Civilian Complaints

Corruption Report Flow

Member becomes aware of corruption/misconduct
   ↓
Report to IAB Command Center (24 hours)
   ↓
Can be anonymous → receive Confidential ID Number
   ↓
Confidential ID Number = duty satisfied

Allegation Against a Member Flow

Allegation made against member
   ↓
Member requests supervisor to respond
   ↓
Supervisor interviews COMPLAINANT
   ↓
Supervisor confers with IAB
   ↓
THEN (and only then) supervisor interviews the MEMBER

Investigating Supervisor Hierarchy

Platoon Commander
   ↓ (unavailable)
Special Operations Lieutenant
   ↓ (unavailable)
Integrity Control Officer (ICO)
   ↓ (unavailable)
CO or Duty Captain

Section 207 — Investigation Procedures

Covers P.G. 207-07, 207-08, 207-10, 207-29, 207-33

P.G. 207-07: Preliminary Investigation of Complaints (Other Than Vice/Narcotics)

7 pages in the Patrol Guide — one of the most critical procedures for supervisors.

Key Definitions

Term Definition
Active Case A case with leads still being pursued or investigative steps remaining
Closed Case A case where all reasonable investigative steps have been completed
Unfounded Case A case where investigation determines that no offense actually occurred

Crime Scene Preservation

Do NOT disturb evidence at the crime scene. This is a fundamental responsibility for all responding members and supervisors.

Patrol Supervisor Notification Requirements

The Patrol Supervisor must PROMPTLY report to the following specialized units:

Unit to Notify Circumstances
Major Case Unit Bank burglary or robbery
Major Case Unit Truck contents valued over $100,000
Major Case Unit Commercial burglary loss over $100,000
Major Case Unit Art theft
Joint Violent Crimes Task Force (JVCTF) Armed bank robberies
Highway District Fatal or serious injury collisions
Special Victims Unit (SVU) ALL sex crimes
Special Victims Unit (SVU) Child abuse involving victims under 11
Special Victims Unit (SVU) Human trafficking

Exam Alert: Memorize the $100,000 threshold for Major Case Unit notifications (truck contents AND commercial burglary).

When to Refer to Detective Squad

Refer a case to the detective squad when any of the following apply:

Condition Details
Serious physical injury Any complaint involving serious physical injury to a victim
Robbery with firearm Or robbery where victim is 60 years or older
Burglary with person present Or burglary loss exceeding $5,000 (or $10,000 in Manhattan)
Unique modus operandi Crime committed with a distinctive or unusual method
Repeat victimization Same victim targeted within 6 months
Perpetrator may be identified Leads exist that could identify the suspect
Similar crimes in vicinity Pattern of related criminal activity
Police impersonation Any crime involving impersonation of a police officer
Identity theft Any complaint involving identity theft
Felonious assault on retail workers Assaults on retail employees in the course of their employment

Exam Alert: The burglary threshold differs by borough: $5,000 citywide, but $10,000 in Manhattan. This Manhattan exception is frequently tested.

P.G. 207-08: Preliminary Investigation of Vice Related, Narcotics, or Organized Crime Complaints

Vice, narcotics, and organized crime complaints follow a separate investigative track routed through the Specialty Enforcement Division. The preliminary investigation procedures differ from standard complaints:

  • Members receiving such complaints ensure initial documentation and notify the appropriate specialized unit.
  • Patrol supervisors coordinate with vice/narcotics units for proper case handling.
  • Exceptions for recording at the precinct of occurrence (pick-up arrests, found drugs) still apply per P.G. 207-01.

P.G. 207-10: Bias Motivated Incidents

5 pages in the Patrol Guide — high priority for the Sergeant exam.

Definition

A Bias Incident is any offense or attempted offense motivated in whole or in part by the offender’s bias based on: - Race - Religion - Ethnicity - Gender - Age - Disability - Sexual orientation (including LGBTQIA+)

Response Chain

The response chain follows a specific escalation path:

1. MOS evaluates the incident for possible bias motivation
   ↓
2. MOS requests PATROL SUPERVISOR to respond
   ↓
3. Patrol Supervisor requests CO/DUTY CAPTAIN
   ↓
4. CO/Duty Captain notifies OPERATIONS UNIT
   ↓
5. Operations Unit issues a BIAS INCIDENT LOG NUMBER

Commanding Officer / Duty Captain Responsibilities

Upon notification of a potential bias incident, the CO or Duty Captain must:

  1. Request Enhanced Crime Team (ECT) to respond
  2. Request detective squad to respond
  3. Prepare an UNUSUAL OCCURRENCE REPORT (PD370-152)

Who Determines Bias Classification

Only the Commanding Officer of the Hate Crime Unit determines whether an incident IS or IS NOT a bias incident, after conferral with the Chief of Detectives.

No other rank or unit makes this final determination.

Commanding Officer of the Command Concerned

  • Must VISIT the scene or location
  • Must PERSONALLY INTERVIEW the victims
  • This is a mandatory, non-delegable responsibility

Reporting Timeline

  • Reports are due within TEN (10) DAYS of the incident

Exam Alert: Three key testable points: (1) Only the Hate Crime Unit CO determines bias classification, (2) the CO of the command concerned must personally interview victims, and (3) the 10-day reporting deadline.

P.G. 207-29: Investigation of Carjackings

Carjacking investigations involve a coordinated response between patrol, the detective squad, and specialized units. Key supervisor responsibilities include:

  • Ensuring immediate notification of the detective squad
  • Coordinating a canvass of the area
  • Broadcasting vehicle and suspect descriptions
  • Ensuring the stolen vehicle alarm is transmitted promptly
  • Coordinating with neighboring commands when the crime occurs near precinct boundaries

P.G. 207-33: Complaints Involving Sex Crimes

4 pages in the Patrol Guide — critical for supervisors.

Core Requirement

The Patrol Supervisor must PROMPTLY report ALL sex crimes to the Special Victims Unit (SVU).

Victim Rights

  • The victim is entitled to have a sexual assault advocate present during any interview.
  • This right must be communicated to the victim.

Hospital Walk-In Sex Crime Complaints

When a sex crime victim presents at a hospital (walk-in): - The 911 dispatcher refers the complaint DIRECTLY to SVU. - Patrol is NOT directed to respond to the hospital for these complaints. - SVU handles hospital walk-in sex crime complaints from initial response onward.

SVU as Central Repository

SVU serves as the central repository for all sex crime information across the Department. All sex crime data is routed through and maintained by SVU.

Exam Alert: The hospital walk-in procedure is a common test question. Remember: 911 sends it directly to SVU, patrol does NOT respond.

Supervisor Quick Reference — Investigation Thresholds

Threshold Triggers
$100,000 Major Case Unit: truck contents, commercial burglary
$10,000 Detective squad referral: burglary in Manhattan
$5,000 Detective squad referral: burglary citywide (except Manhattan)
60+ years old Detective squad referral: robbery victim age
6 months Detective squad referral: same victim re-victimized within this period
Under 11 SVU notification: child abuse victim age
10 days Bias incident report deadline

Section 207 — Missing Persons and Alert Systems

Covers P.G. 207-23, 207-31, 207-39

P.G. 207-23: Missing Persons

7 pages in the Patrol Guide — high priority for the Sergeant exam.

No Minimum Time Requirement

There are NO MINIMUM TIME LIMITS before accepting a missing person report. A report must be taken immediately upon request.

Exam Alert: This is one of the most commonly tested points. There is NO waiting period (no 24-hour rule or any other delay). Accept the report immediately.

Where Missing Person Reports Are Recorded

Missing person reports are recorded at the missing person’s RESIDENT precinct.

Command Responsibility

The Commanding Officer of the residence precinct is ULTIMATELY RESPONSIBLE for the missing person investigation.

Missing Persons from Outside NYC

Reports for persons missing from outside New York City will NOT be accepted. The complainant should be directed to their local police department.

Do Not Refer to Missing Persons Squad

Under NO circumstances should the complainant be referred directly to the Missing Persons Squad. The complaint is always taken at the precinct level first.

Who Can Be a Complainant

The complainant is NOT limited to family members. Any of the following may report a person missing: - Guardian - School representative - Hospital administrator - Roommate - Any other person with knowledge of the missing person’s circumstances

Sergeant Focus: Ensure desk officers and patrol members do not turn away non-family complainants. Anyone with a legitimate concern may file a missing person report.

P.G. 207-31: Silver Alert System

4 pages in the Patrol Guide.

Definition — Vulnerable Senior

A vulnerable senior for Silver Alert purposes is a person who is: - 65 years of age or older with dementia (Alzheimer’s disease or similar condition) - Missing under circumstances indicating imminent danger to their health or safety

Who Can Activate a Silver Alert

ONLY a Captain or above from the Detective Bureau may activate a Silver Alert.

No lower rank and no unit outside the Detective Bureau has this authority.

Timing Requirement

A Silver Alert must be issued within 24 HOURS of the determination that the person qualifies for the alert.

Tactical Consideration

The most prudent approach is to activate the Silver Alert AFTER patrol and field resources have been exhausted. The alert system supplements — rather than replaces — immediate search efforts.

Under-65 Exception

A Silver Alert can also be activated for persons under 65 who have dementia, if the Detective Zone Commanding Officer reasonably believes activation will be beneficial to the investigation.

Exam Alert: Key testable points: (1) Captain or above from Detective Bureau activates, (2) 24-hour window, (3) under-65 exception exists with Detective Zone CO approval.

P.G. 207-39: Blue Alert System

Purpose

The Blue Alert System is activated when a law enforcement officer has been: - Killed in the line of duty, OR - Seriously injured in the line of duty, OR - Missing in the line of duty

AND the suspect has not been apprehended.

Activation

The Blue Alert disseminates critical information to the public and law enforcement to assist in locating the suspect. It is reserved for situations where the threat to officer safety is ongoing.

Supervisor Quick Reference — Missing Persons Decision Tree

Person reported missing
├── Is the missing person a NYC resident?
│   ├── NO → Do NOT accept report; direct to local police
│   └── YES → Accept IMMEDIATELY (no time limit)
│       ├── Record at RESIDENT precinct
│       ├── CO of residence precinct is ULTIMATELY responsible
│       └── Do NOT refer complainant to Missing Persons Squad
│
├── Is the complainant a family member?
│   └── Does NOT matter — anyone can be a complainant
│
