Sergeant Focus Areas

Key Supervisor Procedures

  • P.G. 208-01 — Law of Arrest: Warrant scope (statewide vs county), warrantless arrest standards for petty offenses vs crimes, felony vs misdemeanor pursuit into private residences
  • P.G. 208-02 — Removal to Facility: Supervisor verifies arrest at scene BEFORE removal; designates MOS to document all personnel present and BWC activation
  • P.G. 208-03 — General Processing: High risk prisoner = TWO MOS escort at ALL times; Resisting Recidivist = 2+ qualifying arrests in 5 years; desk officer observes ALL prisoners; juvenile parent/guardian must be present for Miranda
  • P.G. 208-05 — Search Guidelines: Three search levels; strip search requires reasonable suspicion + immediate supervisor approval; CO/Duty Captain resolves disagreements; desk officer has FINAL say on religious head covering
  • P.G. 208-09 — Rights in Custody: Six-part Miranda; cease interrogation on request for attorney or silence; attorney must know prisoner’s identity; muster room interview under continuous observation
  • P.G. 208-24 — Lineups/Showups: Minimum 6 persons; suspect selects position; detective supervisor supervises entire procedure; do NOT advise suspect of right to attorney
  • P.G. 208-27 — DAT General: Class E felonies, misdemeanors, violations eligible; Department REQUIRED to accept listed ID forms; desk officer makes FINAL determination on identity; never hold DAT-eligible prisoner solely for debriefing
  • P.G. 208-36 — Family Offenses/DV: FCA vs NYPD expanded definitions; concurrent jurisdiction (18+, FCA relationship, family offense); interview separately; child abuse failure to report = Class A misdemeanor; crime against child = arrest, no DAT
  • P.G. 208-56 — Foreign Nationals: Mandatory vs non-mandatory notification countries; Intelligence Division Operations Unit handles mandatory notifications
  • P.G. 208-69 — Notifications: Operations Unit direct for government employees, MOS, food stamps >$500; Intelligence Division for UN employees, foreign armed forces, youth gangs
  • P.G. 208-81 — DAT Disqualifiers: Three categories: Condition of Defendant (ID, warrant, FTA 2 years), Specific Charges (family offense, sex crime, hate crime), Order of Protection Needed (27 offenses)
  • P.G. 208-82 — DA Conferral: Same day or next tour; if >4 days, reassign; 1st platoon extends into 2nd platoon

Section 208 — Desk Appearance Ticket (DAT) Procedures

Covers P.G. 208-27, 208-81, 208-82

P.G. 208-27: DAT — General Procedure

What Is a DAT?

A Desk Appearance Ticket (DAT) is an appearance ticket issued in lieu of continued detention after arrest processing. It directs the defendant to appear in court at a later date.

Eligible Offenses

Offense Level DAT Eligible?
Class E Felonies Yes
Misdemeanors Yes
Violations Yes
Class A-D Felonies No

Satisfactory Forms of Identification

The Department is REQUIRED to accept the following forms of ID:

ID Type Notes
NYS Driver’s License —
Passport Any country
U.S. Military ID —
Citizenship Papers —
Out-of-State Driver’s License —
Resident Alien Card —
Municipal ID Including Canadian municipal ID
Public Benefit Card —

Exam Alert: The Department is REQUIRED to accept all of the above. The desk officer makes the FINAL determination on identity, but these specific documents MUST be accepted as satisfactory.

Key ID Rules

Rule Detail
Photo ID requirement NO requirement for photo ID if identity is otherwise verifiable (e.g., personal knowledge of the defendant)
Third party contact Allow defendant to contact a third party to bring identification
Processing delay Delay processing for a reasonable time to allow ID to arrive
Investigation Card An investigation card does NOT preclude DAT issuance
DNA owed DNA owed is NOT a disqualifying factor for DAT

OMNIFORM Monitoring

  • Monitor OMNIFORM DAT Processing screen
  • If no NYSID returned, check back every 30 MINUTES maximum
  • Do not hold prisoner indefinitely waiting for NYSID

Debriefing and DAT

Rule Detail
Desk Officer ensures prisoner is debriefed Standard requirement
DAT-eligible prisoner held solely for debriefing NEVER permitted
Exception Unless there is reasonable suspicion of another crime

Sergeant Focus: A DAT-eligible prisoner should NEVER be held solely for debriefing purposes. The only exception is when there is reasonable suspicion that the prisoner is involved in another crime. Holding a DAT-eligible prisoner without legal basis exposes the Department to liability.

P.G. 208-81: DAT — Disqualifying Factors

Three Categories of Disqualification

Category 1: CONDITION OF DEFENDANT

Factor Detail
Not properly identified Cannot verify identity
Open warrant Active warrant exists
Failure to appear Failed to appear in court in the last 2 YEARS
Physical/mental condition Must confer with Legal Bureau before denying DAT on this basis

Exam Alert: Physical or mental condition alone does not automatically disqualify. The desk officer must CONFER WITH LEGAL BUREAU before using this as a basis to deny a DAT.

Category 2: SPECIFIC CHARGES

Charge Type Examples
Family offense Any family offense charge
Sex crime Article 130 offenses
Hate crime Any bias-motivated offense
Escape/absconding/bail jumping Escape from custody, failure to appear
Weapon on school grounds Criminal possession of a weapon on school property
License-suspendable crime VTL 1192 (DWI), Vehicular Assault, Leaving the Scene of an Accident
A-misdemeanor or felony with harm Involving harm to identifiable person/property AND defendant has pending prosecution or unreturned DAT

Category 3: REASONABLE BELIEF ORDER OF PROTECTION NEEDED

  • Strong possibility of violence against the victim
  • Applies to a list of 27 specific offenses
  • If an Order of Protection may be needed, DAT is not appropriate because the defendant must appear before a judge

Sergeant Focus: The three categories are frequently tested. Remember: Category 1 = who the defendant IS, Category 2 = what the defendant DID, Category 3 = what the defendant MIGHT DO (future danger requiring court protection).

P.G. 208-82: DAT — Conferral with District Attorney

Conferral Timeline

Situation Requirement
Standard Arresting officer MUST confer with DA’s office the SAME DAY or NEXT regularly scheduled tour
Next tour > 4 DAYS away Assign conferral to another officer
1st platoon arrest Arresting officer extends tour into 2nd platoon to confer with DA

Exam Alert: The 4-day rule is critical. If the arresting officer’s next regularly scheduled tour is more than 4 days away, the conferral responsibility must be reassigned. The 1st platoon/2nd platoon extension is also heavily tested — the officer does not wait until a later tour but extends into the next platoon.

Sergeant Focus: Supervisors must track DAT conferrals to ensure they happen within the required timeframe. A missed conferral can result in case dismissal.

Section 208 — Domestic Violence and Family Offenses

Covers P.G. 208-36, 208-37, 208-38, 208-39, 208-70

P.G. 208-36: Family Offenses / Domestic Violence

Family/Household Definitions

Family Court Act (FCA) Definition

A family/household member under the FCA includes: - Persons legally married - Persons formerly married - Persons related by marriage - Persons related by blood - Persons who have a child in common - Persons in an intimate relationship

NYPD Expanded Definition

The NYPD expands the FCA definition to also include: - Persons currently or formerly living together in a family-type relationship

Exam Alert: The NYPD definition is BROADER than the FCA definition. The FCA covers six categories; the NYPD adds cohabitation in a family-type relationship. Know both definitions.

Intimate Relationship Standard

Determined by common sense and totality of circumstances, considering:

Factor Detail
Nature of the relationship Romantic, sexual, emotional bond
Frequency of interaction How often the parties see each other
Duration How long the relationship has lasted

Does NOT qualify: - Casual acquaintance - Business relationship - Educational relationship - Social context only

Family Offenses — Specific Charges

The following offenses qualify as family offenses when committed between family/household members:

Offense Category Specific Charges
Harassment offenses Harassment, Aggravated Harassment
Assault offenses Assault, Menacing, Reckless Endangerment
Sexual offenses Sexual Misconduct, Forcible Touching
Property offenses Criminal Mischief, Grand Larceny
Stalking offenses Stalking (all degrees)
Strangulation Criminal Obstruction of Breathing, Strangulation
Coercion Coercion
Disorderly Conduct Disorderly Conduct
Identity offenses Identity Theft
Image offenses Unlawful Dissemination of Intimate Image

Concurrent Jurisdiction

Concurrent jurisdiction between Criminal Court and Family Court exists when ALL three conditions are met:

Condition Requirement
1 The offense is a family offense (from the list above)
2 The parties have a Family Court Act relationship
3 The offender is 18 years or older

Exception for 16-17 year olds: Concurrent jurisdiction exists ONLY if the 16-17 year old is charged with a felony-level family offense.

If conditions are NOT met: The case proceeds in Criminal Court only.

Exam Alert: All three conditions must be present for concurrent jurisdiction. A common exam trap: a 17-year-old charged with misdemeanor harassment against a spouse — this goes to Criminal Court only because the offender is under 18 and it is not a felony.

Scene Investigation

Requirement Detail
Interview Interview all persons involved SEPARATELY
Photograph Photograph visible injuries and damaged property using smartphone via FORMS application
Interpreters Avoid using family members or bystanders as interpreters
Temporary interpreter use May use temporarily to stabilize the scene
Verification Must verify information with a certified member or Language Line

Child Abuse Reporting

Rule Detail
Mandatory reporting If child abuse is suspected, report is mandatory
Willful failure to report Constitutes a CLASS A MISDEMEANOR
Civil liability Failure to report also creates civil liability
Probable cause crime against child Arrest the perpetrator — NO DAT

Exam Alert: Willful failure to report suspected child abuse is a CLASS A MISDEMEANOR plus civil liability. This is one of the most severe consequences for failure to act in the Patrol Guide. When probable cause exists for a crime against a child, arrest is mandatory and DAT is prohibited.

Mandatory Arrest Rules (from P.G. 208-36)

Felony OR Order of Protection Violation

  • ARREST offender even if complainant/victim requests no arrest
  • OP violation → charge the underlying offense PLUS Criminal Contempt or Aggravated Criminal Contempt
  • All OP violation arrests processed in NYC Criminal Court regardless of issuing court

Misdemeanor (in or out of MOS presence) or Violation (in MOS presence)

  • MUST arrest unless victim on their own initiative states no arrest wanted
  • MOS shall NOT ASK the victim if they want the offender arrested
  • MOS retains discretion to arrest even if victim declines

Exam Alert: Officers must NEVER ask Do you want to press charges? The victim must volunteer this on their own. Even then, the officer retains discretion to arrest.

Factors When Victim Declines Arrest

  • Past history of offender and victim (prior arrests, incidents, injuries)
  • Officer’s observations of scene and victim
  • Statements of witnesses
  • Threats of suicide, homicide, or future violence
  • Threatened use of weapons, presence/access to weapons
  • Mental/physical state of offender (drugs, alcohol)
  • Presence of other at-risk household members (including elderly)

If ANY doubt about safety → AN ARREST SHOULD BE EFFECTED

Cross-Complaint — Primary Physical Aggressor

When probable cause exists that more than one family member committed a misdemeanor:

Determination Criteria

  1. Comparative extent of injuries
  2. Whether any party is threatening future harm
  3. Prior history of domestic violence
  4. Whether any party acted defensively

Procedure

  1. Attempt to identify primary physical aggressor
  2. Confer with patrol supervisor
  3. Arrest the primary physical aggressor
  4. If unable to determine → may arrest both parties
  5. Document all factors in Activity Log and Complaint Report

Exam Alert: Even when unable to determine the primary aggressor, it is lawful to arrest both parties. The primary consideration is always prevention of further violence and safety of ALL household members.

Order of Protection — Verification and Enforcement

Verification Methods (in order)

  1. Request complainant produce the OP
  2. Query eJusticeNY application (desktop or smartphone Portal app)
  3. Query zFINEST (name search — info only, no copy produced)
  4. Contact Central Records Division, Identification Unit by phone

Out-of-State Orders (Violence Against Women Act)

Enforce if: - Order appears valid on its face (not expired, signed by judge) - Probable cause of violation exists - Offender had notice of the order - Offender had opportunity to be heard

All Cases — Mandatory Actions

Action Detail
DV Hotline Provide (800) 621-HOPE (4673)
NYS Domestic Incident Report Prepare DCJS-3221 in ALL family/household incidents
Firearms inquiry MUST ask about access to guns in residence or other locations
Interview separately Interview all persons separately
Photographs Take via FORMS for visible injuries/damaged property
Victim copies Give pink copies of DIR and Victim Rights Notice
Radio disposition Use 10-90F, 10-92F, 10-93F codes

P.G. 208-70: Processing of NYS Domestic Incident Reports

  • Hard copy required for all founded DV incidents
  • No hard copy for unfounded incidents within NYC (enter directly into FORMS)
  • Hard copy required for incidents outside NYC but within NYS

Sergeant Focus: Strangulation injuries are often internal and not immediately visible. Supervisors must ensure victims receive medical evaluation even when external injuries are absent.

Section 208 — Law of Arrest and General Processing

Covers P.G. 208-01, 208-02, 208-03, 208-09, 208-10, 208-11, 208-12, 208-13, 208-15, 208-16, 208-17

P.G. 208-01: Law of Arrest

Arrest WITH Warrant

Warrant Issued By Scope of Execution
NYC Criminal Court, District Court, or Superior Court (addressed to MOS/Department) Arrest ANYTIME, ANYWHERE in New York State
City, Town, or Village Court Arrest within county of issuance or ADJOINING county only

Exam Alert: The key distinction is the issuing court. A NYC Criminal Court warrant has statewide reach; a city/town/village court warrant is limited to the county of issuance and adjoining counties.

Arrest WITHOUT Warrant

Situation Standard Required Geographical Limitation
Any offense committed in MOS’s presence Reasonable cause Geographical area of employment
Crime (felony or misdemeanor) — in presence or NOT Reasonable cause Geographical area of employment
Petty offense (violation or traffic infraction) In MOS’s presence AND reasonable cause Within geographical area of employment AND county of occurrence or adjoining county

Sergeant Focus: Petty offenses have the most restrictive requirements — the offense must be in the officer’s presence, within the geographical area of employment, AND within the county of occurrence or adjoining county. Crimes (felonies and misdemeanors) require only reasonable cause and have no county limitation within the geographical area.

Close Pursuit

Offense Type Pursuit Scope
Petty offenses May pursue into any part of the State
Crimes May pursue outside the State

Entry into Private Residence

Suspect Category Rules for Entry
Felony suspect fleeing into private residence May pursue into the residence
Misdemeanor suspect fleeing into private residence May enter ONLY to: prevent imminent injury, prevent destruction of evidence, prevent escape, or under exigent circumstances; otherwise need warrant or consent

Exam Alert: For misdemeanor suspects, mere flight into a residence is NOT sufficient justification for entry. One of the four specific conditions must exist, or the officer must obtain a warrant or consent.

P.G. 208-02: Arrests — Removal to Facility

Supervisor Verification at Scene

  • Supervisor verifies arrest at scene BEFORE removal to facility
  • Immediate supervisor designates MOS at scene to obtain names/tax numbers of ALL MOS present
  • Designated MOS ascertains BWC activation for all MOS at scene

Preferred Name

  • PREFERRED NAME: The name an arrestee prefers to be called, based on their gender identity
  • Officers must ascertain and use the arrestee’s preferred name

Child Present at Arrest

  • Immediately ascertain if a CHILD IS PRESENT
  • Effect arrest outside child’s sight and hearing if possible
  • Do NOT display a firearm in front of a child unless necessary for safety

Medical/Psychological Inquiry

  • Immediately inquire about medical or psychological treatment
  • BWC must be activated during this inquiry

Handcuffing

  • Handcuff with hands behind back (standard procedure)
  • Pregnant prisoners: Rear cuff absent exceptional circumstances only (i.e., rear cuffing is generally avoided for pregnant prisoners; use rear cuffing only when exceptional circumstances require it)

Sergeant Focus: The supervisor’s verification at the scene BEFORE removal is a critical checkpoint. The supervisor must also ensure a member is designated to document all MOS present and BWC activation status.

P.G. 208-03: Arrests — General Processing

Immediate Actions Upon Arrest

  1. Field search/frisk prisoner immediately
  2. Advise Miranda rights BEFORE any questioning
  3. Transport to facility for processing

Juvenile Arrest Processing

Requirement Rule
Notification Notify Juvenile Strategies Unit (24/7) AND parent/guardian IMMEDIATELY
Miranda Parent/guardian must be present for Miranda warnings
Parent objects to questioning NO questioning even if juvenile is willing

Exam Alert: If a parent or guardian objects to questioning, questioning MUST stop — even if the juvenile wants to answer questions. The parent’s objection overrides the juvenile’s willingness.

Desk Officer Responsibilities

Duty Details
Escape risk assessment Determine escape risk for every prisoner
High risk prisoner Assign TWO MOS to escort prisoner at ALL times
Physical/mental condition Observe condition of ALL prisoners entering command
Prisoner debriefing Ensure prisoner is debriefed
Dependent inquiry Inquire about dependent adult/child at prisoner’s residence

Resisting Recidivist

A Resisting Recidivist is a person arrested for any of the following charges TWO or more times within the PAST 5 YEARS: - Resisting Arrest - Assault 2nd Degree (subdivision 3) - Assault on a Police Officer - Aggravated Assault on a Police Officer

Action: Enhance processing with the detective squad when a resisting recidivist is identified.

Property Removal from Prisoner

Remove the following from the prisoner: - Unlawfully carried items - Evidence of crime - Dangerous items: belts, shoelaces, drawstrings, glass items - Prescription drugs (safeguard and document)

Sergeant Focus: The desk officer must personally observe the physical and mental condition of ALL prisoners entering the command. High-risk prisoners require TWO MOS escort at ALL times — not just during transport.

P.G. 208-09: Rights of Persons in Custody

Miranda Warnings — Six Parts

# Warning
1 You have the right to remain silent
2 Anything you say may be used against you
3 You have the right to an attorney before and during questioning
4 An attorney will be provided if you cannot afford one
5 You have the right to remain silent until you consult with an attorney
6 Are you willing to answer questions?

Cease Interrogation

  • Immediately cease interrogation if the subject:
    • Requests an attorney, OR
    • Expresses a wish to remain silent

Attorney Access

Situation Rule
Attorney requests interview Desk Officer permits interview in muster room for a reasonable time
During interview Assign MOS to keep prisoner and attorney under continuous observation
Objects passed Ensure no objects are passed between prisoner and attorney
Attorney does NOT know prisoner’s identity Attorney is NOT permitted to interview the prisoner

Exam Alert: An attorney who cannot identify the prisoner by name is NOT permitted to conduct an interview. The attorney must know who they are there to see.

P.G. 208-10: Prisoner Identification and Processing

Covers procedures for proper identification of prisoners during arrest processing, including fingerprinting, photographing, and NYSID verification.

Sergeant Focus: Ensure arresting officers follow identification procedures thoroughly. Misidentification can lead to wrongful release or processing errors.

P.G. 208-11: Arrest Processing — Felony Complaints

Covers the specific processing requirements for felony arrests, including: - Preparation of felony complaint - Coordination with the District Attorney’s office - Timely arraignment requirements - Evidence documentation standards

Sergeant Focus: Supervisors must monitor felony processing timelines closely to ensure arraignment deadlines are met.

P.G. 208-12: Arrest Processing — Misdemeanor Complaints

Covers processing requirements for misdemeanor arrests, including: - Preparation of misdemeanor complaint - Determination of DAT eligibility vs. continued custody - Coordination with court sections

P.G. 208-13: Arrest Processing — Violation Complaints

Covers processing for violation-level offenses, including: - DAT issuance considerations - Simplified processing procedures - Release protocols

P.G. 208-15: Arrests — Warrant Execution

Covers procedures for executing arrest warrants, including: - Verification of warrant validity - Proper identification of the subject - Coordination between commands - Documentation requirements

P.G. 208-16: Arrests — Surrender of Wanted Persons

Covers the procedures when a wanted person voluntarily surrenders, including: - Processing at the command of surrender - Notification to the warrant-issuing command - Documentation requirements

P.G. 208-17: Arrests — Pick-Up Arrests

Covers procedures when a person wanted by another command or agency is apprehended: - Verification of warrant/wanted status - Notification to the requesting command/agency - Transfer of custody protocols - Documentation requirements

Sergeant Focus: Supervisors must verify the validity of the warrant or wanted status before authorizing transfer of custody.

P.G. 208-24 — Identification Lineups/Showups

Date Issued: 06/23/20 | Date Effective: 06/23/20 | Pages: 1–3

Definitions

Term Definition
Identification Lineup Placing a criminal suspect with at least five other persons for identification by victim/witnesses
Identification Showup Prompt, on-the-scene presentation of a suspect singly (one-to-one display) for expeditious identification and/or early release of an innocent suspect

Key Procedures

Before the Lineup

Step Rule
Doubt about legality Resolve by conferring with patrol supervisor/desk officer
Arrest requirement Suspect must be arrested before forced lineup; voluntary consent does not require arrest
When NOT needed Witness/victim and perpetrator are known to each other (relatives, close acquaintances) OR perpetrator caught in the act by an officer
Miranda Give warnings only if suspect will be interrogated before, during, or after
Inform suspect Will appear in lineup for identification purposes
Right to attorney Do NOT advise suspect of right to attorney for lineup
If attorney requested Inform suspect there is no right to a lawyer for lineup

Detective Supervisor Responsibilities

The detective supervisor (or qualified investigative unit supervisor) must:

  1. Personally supervise the entire lineup procedure
  2. Interview witnesses separately before lineup
  3. Obtain and record description of suspect from each witness
  4. Prevent lineup participants from being seen by witnesses beforehand
  5. Record all details: utterances, actions, witness responses, names/addresses of all present
  6. Prepare diagram with circles for each position
  7. Instruct witness to indicate circle — not comment on recognition
  8. Mark and retain diagram after identification
  9. Complete LINEUP REPORT (PD373-141)

Lineup Composition Requirements

Requirement Standard
Minimum participants Suspect + at least 5 others (minimum 6 total)
Multiple suspects Separate lineup for each suspect
Fillers Use different fillers for each lineup
Participants must be Same sex and race as suspect
Physical similarity Approximately same age, height, and physical makeup
Clothing Similarly clothed; no distinctive police uniform parts
Distinctive clothing Cover unusual/distinctive clothing worn by suspect

Minors in Lineups (Under 18 as Non-Suspect Participants)

  • Obtain written permission from parent/legal guardian on CONSENT FORM (PD 377-030)
  • Isolate minors from harmful influences
  • Have precinct youth coordination officer present if possible
  • Prevent photographs from being used for non-evidence purposes

Attorney at Lineup

Situation Rule
Attorney present at site May observe lineup from room or any place where attorney cannot be seen
Attorney contacts police Delay lineup for reasonable time to permit attorney to appear
Reasonable delay factors Would the delay cause significant inconvenience to witness OR undermine advantages of prompt identification?
Attorney talking to witnesses NOT permitted
Attorney suggestions Direct to officer conducting lineup; consider if reasonable and practical
Attorney interference NOT permitted

Sergeant Study Notes

Key Numbers to Memorize

  • 6 = minimum persons in a lineup (suspect + 5 others)
  • 1 = showup is a one-to-one display
  • 1 = separate lineup per suspect
  • Different fillers for each separate lineup

Common Exam Angles

  1. How many people must be in a lineup? → At least 6 (suspect + 5 others)
  2. Who supervises the lineup? → Detective supervisor or qualified investigative unit supervisor
  3. Does the suspect have a right to an attorney at a lineup? → NO
  4. When is a lineup NOT necessary? → When victim/perpetrator know each other or caught in the act
  5. Can the attorney observe? → Yes, but cannot talk to witnesses or interfere

Section 208 — Notification Requirements

Covers P.G. 208-69

P.G. 208-69: Notifications

Operations Unit — DIRECT Notification Required

The following arrest types require direct notification to the Operations Unit:

Category Specific Situations
Alcohol Illegal alcohol manufacture
Food stamps Federal food stamps valued at >$500
Government employees NYC, federal, or state employee arrests
Members of the Service Any MOS arrest
Tax offenses Cigarette tax violations
Prescription fraud Forged narcotics prescriptions
Tax-related crimes Extortion or bribery related to taxes
Identity documents Multiple blank or different Social Security cards
Parking meters Parking meter larceny
Postal violations Any postal-related offense
Military Armed Forces personnel, AWOL, or deserter
Health impersonation DOH (Department of Health) impersonation

Exam Alert: The $500 threshold for federal food stamps is a frequently tested number. MOS arrests ALWAYS require Operations Unit notification.

Direct Notification to Specialized Units

Unit to Notify Trigger Situations
Intelligence Division Criminal anarchy, anarchistic literature, UN employee arrest, foreign armed forces member, youth gang activity
Port Authority Offenses involving Port Authority jurisdiction
TBTA (Triborough Bridge and Tunnel Authority) Offenses involving TBTA facilities
NYS DOCCS (Dept of Corrections and Community Supervision) Parole violator arrests
NYS Police Offenses requiring state police notification
NYS Court System Offenses involving court personnel or on court property
School Safety Division Offenses involving DOE (Department of Education) facilities or personnel
Citywide Vandals Task Force Graffiti offenses
CUNY Offenses involving City University of New York facilities or personnel

Sergeant Focus: Supervisors must ensure all required notifications are made promptly. Failure to notify specialized units can compromise investigations and inter-agency relationships. The Intelligence Division notification list is particularly important — UN employees, foreign armed forces, and criminal anarchy all require immediate Intelligence Division contact.

Quick Reference: Notification Categories

If the arrest involves… Notify…
Any government employee (city/state/federal) Operations Unit
Any MOS Operations Unit
Foreign armed forces or UN employee Intelligence Division
Youth gang activity Intelligence Division
School (DOE) School Safety Division
Graffiti Citywide Vandals Task Force
Parole violator NYS DOCCS
Postal violation Operations Unit
Food stamps >$500 Operations Unit

Section 208 — Search Guidelines

Covers P.G. 208-05

P.G. 208-05: General Search Guidelines

Three Levels of Search

Level 1: FRISK / FIELD SEARCH

Aspect Detail
What External body examination
When Immediately after apprehension
Timing Before or after rear handcuffing
Special attention areas Waistband, armpit, collar, groin
Who performs Arresting or assisting officer

Level 2: SEARCH AT POLICE FACILITY

Aspect Detail
What Thorough search with removal of outer garments
Who performs SAME GENDER member
NOT the same as Strip search — this is a more thorough pat-down with outer garment removal
Gender not apparent or arrestee objects Assign gender requested by arrestee (consistent with officer safety and resource availability)
Critical rule NEVER search a person to determine their gender

Exam Alert: A search at the police facility is NOT a strip search. It involves removal of outer garments but not undergarments. The distinction between Level 2 and Level 3 is frequently tested.

Level 3: STRIP SEARCH

Aspect Detail
Routine use May NOT be conducted routinely
Authorization required Knowledge and approval of arresting officer’s IMMEDIATE SUPERVISOR or borough court section supervisor
Standard REASONABLE SUSPICION that weapons, contraband, or evidence are concealed on the person
Who performs Same gender member
Location Secure area with utmost privacy

Strip Search Disagreement Resolution

Situation Resolution
Disagreement between desk officer and outside supervisor Desk officer notifies CO/Duty Captain
CO/Duty Captain Makes the FINAL decision on whether strip search is conducted

Exam Alert: The CO or Duty Captain has the FINAL decision authority when there is a disagreement about conducting a strip search. This is a key supervisor responsibility.

Religious Head Covering

  • Removal may be required during search
  • If disagreement arises about removal → the Desk Officer makes the FINAL determination

Sergeant Focus: The desk officer’s authority over religious head covering decisions is absolute at the command level. This mirrors the desk officer’s broad authority over prisoner processing decisions within the command.

Strip Search — Factors Considered for Authorization

When determining whether reasonable suspicion exists for a strip search, consider: - Nature of the crime (serious violent felony) - Arrest circumstances - Subject’s reputation (extremely violent person) - Acts of violence - Unaccounted hits on magnetometers or walk-through metal detectors - Discoveries or information from previous searches of same individual or co-arrestees

ABSOLUTE PROHIBITION: Body Cavity Search

  • UNDER NO CONDITIONS shall any MOS conduct a body cavity search
  • If an object or foreign material is visually detected within any body cavity during a strip search → notify desk officer immediately
  • It should NOT be necessary to touch the body, except for hair examination

Subsequent Strip Search

A subsequent strip search may NOT be conducted unless there is reasonable belief the subject has acquired a weapon or contraband since the prior search.

Mobility Disability Considerations

Step Action
1 Ask consent to remove arrestee from assistive device for search
2 If consent given but arrestee cannot lift themselves → dispatch EMS
3 If arrestee refuses → Contact Legal Bureau for guidance
4 Transport to ADA-compliant precinct

Sergeant Focus: Supervisors must ensure that strip searches are NEVER conducted routinely. Every strip search requires (1) reasonable suspicion, (2) immediate supervisor or borough court section supervisor approval, and (3) same-gender officer in a private, secure location. Document the basis for reasonable suspicion. NEVER allow a body cavity search under any circumstances.

Section 208 — Special Arrest Procedures

Covers P.G. 208-04, 208-14, 208-20, 208-22, 208-23, 208-24, 208-25, 208-26, 208-34, 208-35, 208-40, 208-41, 208-42, 208-44, 208-46, 208-47, 208-48, 208-49, 208-50, 208-52, 208-53, 208-54, 208-55, 208-56, 208-57, 208-58, 208-59, 208-60, 208-61, 208-62, 208-66, 208-67, 208-68, 208-71, 208-72, 208-73, 208-74, 208-75, 208-78, 208-79, 208-80, 208-83, 208-84

P.G. 208-04: Arrest by Civilian

Key Rules

Rule Detail
When a civilian may arrest For an OFFENSE committed in their presence
MOS receiving custody Must determine if legal basis exists for the civilian arrest
If legal basis exists Process the arrest; the civilian is the complainant
If NO legal basis Do NOT process; advise the civilian of their options

Sergeant Focus: The supervisor must verify that the civilian’s arrest meets the legal standard — the offense must have been committed in the civilian’s presence. If the legal basis is questionable, consult with Legal Bureau.

P.G. 208-14: Arrests — Off-Duty Members

Covers procedures and responsibilities for off-duty members effecting arrests, including notification requirements and processing protocols.

P.G. 208-20: Arrest Processing — Intoxicated Drivers

Covers DWI/DWAI arrest processing, including chemical test procedures, refusal documentation, and VTL 1192 processing requirements.

P.G. 208-22: Arrest Processing — Desk Officer Responsibilities

Covers the desk officer’s comprehensive responsibilities during arrest processing, including prisoner welfare, paperwork review, and arraignment coordination.

P.G. 208-23: Arrest Processing — Online Booking System (OLBS)

Covers procedures for using the Online Booking System for arrest processing, including data entry requirements and system protocols.

P.G. 208-24: Lineups and Showups

Lineup Requirements

Requirement Detail
Minimum participants Suspect + at least 5 OTHER persons = minimum 6 total
Composition Same sex, race; approximately same age, height, physical makeup
Clothing Participants must be similarly clothed
Position selection Permit the suspect to SELECT their position in the lineup
Supervision Detective supervisor personally supervises the entire procedure

Showup Requirements

Requirement Detail
Definition A prompt, on-the-scene, singly, one-to-one display of a suspect to a witness
When used Close in time and proximity to the crime

Critical Lineup Rules

Rule Detail
Arrest required Suspect must be ARRESTED before being forced into a lineup (unless voluntary consent)
Known parties Lineup is NOT necessary when victim and perpetrator are KNOWN to each other
Right to attorney Do NOT advise suspect of right to attorney for lineup
Witness separation Prevent witnesses from speaking to each other before, during, or after the lineup

Exam Alert: Two counter-intuitive rules: (1) Do NOT advise the suspect of right to attorney for a lineup, and (2) the suspect SELECTS their own position. Both are frequently tested.

P.G. 208-25: Arrest Processing — Multiple Defendants

Covers procedures when processing arrests involving multiple defendants from a single incident.

P.G. 208-26: Arrest Processing — Juvenile Offenders

Covers specialized processing requirements for juvenile offenders, coordinating with P.G. 215 (Juvenile Matters).

P.G. 208-34: Arrests — Organized Crime Offenses

Covers arrest procedures for organized crime-related offenses, including coordination with specialized units and intelligence sharing protocols.

P.G. 208-35: Arrests — Prostitution-Related Offenses

Covers procedures for prostitution and sex trafficking arrests, including victim identification protocols and specialized unit notifications.

P.G. 208-40: Family Offenses — Victim Notification

Covers mandatory notifications to victims in family offense cases.

P.G. 208-41: Family Offenses — Reporting Requirements

Covers documentation and reporting requirements for family offense incidents.

P.G. 208-42: Arrests — Hate Crimes

Covers enhanced procedures for bias-motivated crime arrests, including: - Coordination with the Hate Crime Unit - Enhanced documentation requirements - Victim services referral

P.G. 208-44: Arrests — Sex Offenses

Covers arrest procedures for sex offense cases, including: - SVU notification - Victim rights and services - Evidence preservation - Medical examination coordination

P.G. 208-46: Arrests — School-Related Incidents

Covers arrest procedures occurring on school grounds or involving school-related incidents, including School Safety Division coordination.

P.G. 208-47: Arrests — Taxi/Livery Related Offenses

Covers procedures for arrests involving taxi and livery vehicle operators or passengers.

P.G. 208-48: Arrests — Identity Theft

Covers arrest procedures for identity theft cases, including evidence preservation and victim notification requirements.

P.G. 208-49: Arrests — Human Trafficking

Covers procedures for human trafficking arrests, including victim identification, specialized unit notification, and victim services coordination.

P.G. 208-50: Arrests — Robbery Patterns

Covers arrest procedures when a robbery pattern has been identified, including detective bureau coordination.

P.G. 208-52: Arrests — Strangulation/Criminal Obstruction of Breathing

Covers enhanced arrest procedures for strangulation offenses, including medical evaluation requirements and evidence documentation.

P.G. 208-53: Arrests — Stalking Offenses

Covers procedures for stalking-related arrests, including documentation of the course of conduct and technology-facilitated stalking.

P.G. 208-54: Arrests — Mental Health Related

Covers procedures when an arrest involves a person in mental health crisis, including EDP protocols and coordination with medical personnel.

P.G. 208-55: Arrests — Terrorism/National Security

Covers arrest procedures involving terrorism-related offenses, including Intelligence Division notification and evidence handling protocols.

P.G. 208-56: Foreign Nationals

Key Definitions

Term Definition
Foreign national Any person who is NOT a United States citizen, regardless of immigration status

Notification Requirements

Category Action Required
Mandatory notification countries Contact Intelligence Division Operations Unit (24 hours) — they handle consular notification
Non-mandatory notification countries INFORM the prisoner of their right to have their embassy/consulate notified

Exam Alert: For mandatory notification countries, the Department notifies the consulate regardless of the prisoner’s wishes. For non-mandatory countries, the prisoner has the RIGHT to notification but can decline. The Intelligence Division Operations Unit handles mandatory notifications.

Sergeant Focus: Supervisors must determine the citizenship status of all prisoners and ensure proper consular notification. Failure to provide mandatory notification can have diplomatic consequences and may compromise prosecution.

P.G. 208-57: Arrests — Military Personnel

Covers procedures for arresting active-duty military personnel, including military authority notification requirements.

P.G. 208-58: Arrests — Diplomatic/Consular Immunity

Covers procedures when a person claiming diplomatic or consular immunity is arrested, including verification protocols and Intelligence Division notification.

P.G. 208-59: Arrests — Federal Employees

Covers procedures for arresting federal employees, including notification requirements to the relevant federal agency.

P.G. 208-60: Arrests — Members of the Service

Covers procedures when a member of the NYPD is arrested, including: - Immediate notification to Operations Unit - IAB notification - Firearm/shield surrender - Special processing requirements

P.G. 208-61: Arrests — Other Law Enforcement Officers

Covers procedures when a member of another law enforcement agency is arrested, including notification to the officer’s agency.

P.G. 208-62: Arrests — Attorneys

Covers procedures for arresting attorneys, including notification requirements and special processing considerations.

P.G. 208-66: Arrests — Parole/Probation Violators

Covers procedures for arresting persons who have violated parole or probation, including notification to NYS DOCCS or Probation Department.

P.G. 208-67: Arrests — Fugitives from Other Jurisdictions

Covers procedures for apprehending fugitives wanted by other states or jurisdictions, including extradition processing.

P.G. 208-68: Arrests — Unlawful Assembly/Demonstrations

Covers mass arrest procedures during unlawful assembly or demonstration situations, including field processing and documentation requirements.

P.G. 208-71: Arrests — Firearms/Weapons Offenses

Covers enhanced procedures for weapons arrests, including: - Firearms tracing - ATF notification - Evidence handling for weapons

P.G. 208-72: Arrests — Narcotics Offenses

Covers narcotics arrest procedures, including evidence handling, lab submission, and specialized unit coordination.

P.G. 208-73: Arrests — Graffiti/Vandalism

Covers arrest procedures for graffiti and vandalism offenses, including Citywide Vandals Task Force notification.

P.G. 208-74: Arrests — Grand Jury Notification

Covers procedures for notifying the grand jury regarding felony arrests within required timeframes.

P.G. 208-75: Arrests — Sealing of Records

Covers procedures related to sealed arrest records, including when records must be sealed and restrictions on access.

P.G. 208-78: Arrests — Transit System Offenses

Covers arrest procedures for offenses occurring within the transit system, including Transit Bureau coordination.

P.G. 208-79: Arrests — NYCHA/Housing Authority

Covers arrest procedures for offenses occurring on NYCHA property, including Housing Bureau coordination.

P.G. 208-80: Arrests — Bench Warrant Processing

Covers procedures for processing bench warrants, including verification and prisoner transport to court.

P.G. 208-83: Religious Head Covering During Arrest Processing

Key Rule

Situation Resolution
Disagreement about removal of religious head covering during processing Desk Officer makes the FINAL determination

Sergeant Focus: This mirrors the search guidelines in P.G. 208-05. The desk officer has ultimate authority within the command on religious head covering decisions during arrest processing.

P.G. 208-84: Early Diversion Initiative (Project Reset)

Eligibility

Criterion Requirement
Age 18 years or older
Criminal history First-time offenders
Charge type Non-violent misdemeanors

Purpose

Project Reset provides an alternative to traditional arrest processing by diverting eligible defendants to community-based programming, reducing the burden on the court system while providing appropriate intervention.

Sergeant Focus: Supervisors should ensure that eligible defendants are identified for Project Reset during processing. The program criteria are narrow — age 18+, first-time offender, non-violent misdemeanor only.

Section 208 — Key Terms, Forms, and Abbreviations

Core Arrest Definitions

Term Definition Source
Arrest The taking of a person into custody in order that they may be held to answer for a criminal offense or violation P.G. 208-01
Pick-Up Arrest An arrest of a person wanted by another command or agency P.G. 208-17
Preferred Name The name an arrestee prefers to be called, based on their gender identity P.G. 208-02
Probable Cause Facts and circumstances that would lead a reasonable person to believe a crime has been, is being, or is about to be committed P.G. 208-01
Reasonable Suspicion (for strip search) Articulable facts supporting a belief that weapons, contraband, or evidence are concealed on the person; required standard for authorizing a strip search P.G. 208-05

Search Levels

Term Definition Source
Frisk / Field Search External body examination conducted immediately after apprehension; special attention to waistband, armpit, collar, groin P.G. 208-05
Search at Police Facility Thorough search by same-gender member with removal of outer garments; NOT the same as a strip search P.G. 208-05
Strip Search Search requiring removal of undergarments; may NOT be conducted routinely; requires reasonable suspicion and approval of arresting officer’s immediate supervisor or borough court section supervisor; same gender, secure area, utmost privacy P.G. 208-05

Prisoner Classification

Term Definition Source
High Risk Prisoner A prisoner determined by the desk officer to be an escape risk; requires TWO MOS assigned to escort at ALL times P.G. 208-03
Resisting Recidivist A person arrested for Resisting Arrest, Assault 2nd (sub 3), Assault on PO, or Aggravated Assault on PO, 2 or more times within the past 5 years; processing enhanced with detective squad P.G. 208-03

DAT-Related Terms

Term Definition Source
DAT (Desk Appearance Ticket) An appearance ticket issued in lieu of continued detention for Class E felonies, misdemeanors, and violations; directs defendant to appear in court at a later date P.G. 208-27
Satisfactory Form of Identification NYS license, passport, US military ID, citizenship papers, out-of-state license, resident alien card, Municipal ID (including Canadian), Public Benefit Card; Department REQUIRED to accept these forms P.G. 208-27

Miranda Warnings — Six Parts

# Warning Text Source
1 You have the right to remain silent P.G. 208-09
2 Anything you say may be used against you P.G. 208-09
3 You have the right to an attorney before and during questioning P.G. 208-09
4 An attorney will be provided if you cannot afford one P.G. 208-09
5 You have the right to remain silent until you consult with an attorney P.G. 208-09
6 Are you willing to answer questions? P.G. 208-09

Domestic Violence / Family Offense Terms

Term Definition Source
Family/Household (FCA Definition) Legally married, formerly married, related by marriage, related by blood, child in common, intimate relationship P.G. 208-36
Family/Household (NYPD Expanded) FCA definition PLUS persons currently or formerly living together in a family-type relationship P.G. 208-36
Family Offense Specific offenses committed between family/household members, including harassment, assault, disorderly conduct, stalking, menacing, sexual misconduct, criminal mischief, strangulation, identity theft, grand larceny, coercion, unlawful dissemination of intimate image P.G. 208-36
Concurrent Jurisdiction When both Criminal Court and Family Court have jurisdiction; requires: (1) family offense, (2) FCA relationship, (3) offender 18+ (or 16-17 charged with felony-level family offense) P.G. 208-36
Order of Protection A court order directing a person to refrain from certain conduct toward a protected party; violation may be a mandatory arrest P.G. 208-37

Identification Procedures

Term Definition Source
Lineup An identification procedure where a suspect is placed among at least 5 other persons (minimum 6 total); same sex, race, approximate age/height/build; suspect selects position; detective supervisor supervises P.G. 208-24
Showup A prompt, on-the-scene, singly, one-to-one display of a suspect to a witness; used close in time and proximity to the crime P.G. 208-24

Foreign National Terms

Term Definition Source
Foreign National Any person who is NOT a United States citizen, regardless of immigration status P.G. 208-56
Mandatory Notification Countries Countries requiring automatic consular notification upon arrest of their citizen, handled through the Intelligence Division Operations Unit (24 hrs) P.G. 208-56

Forms

Form Name Form Number Purpose Reference
OLBS Worksheet PD244-159 Online Booking System worksheet for arrest processing P.G. 208-03
Arrest Supplement PD244-157 Supplemental arrest information and documentation P.G. 208-03
Desk Appearance Ticket PD260-121 Appearance ticket issued in lieu of detention P.G. 208-27
Investigation Card PD373-163 Record of field investigation; does NOT preclude DAT issuance P.G. 208-27

Units and Abbreviations

Abbreviation Full Name Context
BWC Body-Worn Camera Must be activated during medical/psychological inquiry and at arrest scene
OLBS Online Booking System Primary arrest processing system
NYSID New York State Identification Number Fingerprint-based identification; check every 30 min max during DAT processing
DA District Attorney Conferral required same day or next tour for DAT cases
FCA Family Court Act Defines family/household relationships for concurrent jurisdiction
SVU Special Victims Unit Notified for sex offenses and child abuse
DOCCS Department of Corrections and Community Supervision Notified for parole violator arrests
TBTA Triborough Bridge and Tunnel Authority Notified for offenses at TBTA facilities
CUNY City University of New York Notified for offenses at CUNY facilities
DOE Department of Education School Safety Division notified for DOE-related offenses
VTL Vehicle and Traffic Law VTL 1192 (DWI) is a DAT disqualifier

Critical Numbers

Number What It Represents Source
6 Minimum total persons in a lineup (suspect + 5 others) P.G. 208-24
2 years Failed to appear in court — DAT disqualifier P.G. 208-81
5 years Lookback period for resisting recidivist classification (2+ arrests) P.G. 208-03
30 minutes Maximum interval for NYSID recheck during DAT processing P.G. 208-27
4 days If next tour > 4 days away, reassign DA conferral to another officer P.G. 208-82
$500 Federal food stamp threshold for Operations Unit notification P.G. 208-69
27 Number of specific offenses on the Order of Protection needed list (DAT disqualifier Category 3) P.G. 208-81

Section 208 — Review Questions

25 multiple-choice questions covering key Sergeant exam topics from Section 208.

Question 1

A sergeant receives a warrant issued by a city court in Westchester County. The suspect is located in a neighboring county. Can the warrant be executed there?

A. No, city court warrants can only be executed within the city limits B. Yes, city/town/village court warrants may be executed in the county of issuance or adjoining county C. Yes, all warrants have statewide reach D. No, the warrant must be re-issued by the adjoining county’s court

Answer B. Per P.G. 208-01, warrants issued by a city, town, or village court may be executed within the county of issuance OR an adjoining county. Only warrants from NYC Criminal Court, district court, or superior court have statewide authority.

Question 2

An officer observes a traffic infraction in an adjoining county while off-duty. The officer is employed in Kings County. Can the officer make a warrantless arrest?

A. Yes, any offense in the officer’s presence allows arrest B. Yes, but only if the infraction is also a misdemeanor C. No, petty offenses require the arrest to be within the geographical area of employment AND the county of occurrence or adjoining county D. No, off-duty officers cannot make warrantless arrests

Answer C. Per P.G. 208-01, warrantless arrest for a petty offense (violation or traffic infraction) requires the offense be in the officer’s presence, within the geographical area of employment, AND within the county of occurrence or adjoining county. An officer in an adjoining county outside their area of employment cannot make this arrest.

Question 3

A misdemeanor suspect flees into a private residence. Under what circumstance may an officer enter without a warrant or consent?

A. Whenever the officer has probable cause for the misdemeanor B. Only to prevent imminent injury, destruction of evidence, escape, or under exigent circumstances C. Never — a warrant is always required for a misdemeanor suspect D. Only if the suspect is also wanted on a felony warrant

Answer B. Per P.G. 208-01, for a misdemeanor suspect fleeing into a private residence, officers may enter ONLY to prevent imminent injury, prevent destruction of evidence, prevent escape, or under exigent circumstances. For a felony suspect, officers may pursue directly into the residence without these additional requirements.

Question 4

During a search at a police facility, the arrestee’s gender is not apparent. How should the searching officer’s gender be determined?

A. The desk officer assigns the searching officer based on the arrestee’s biological sex B. A supervisor makes the determination based on visual assessment C. The search is assigned to the gender requested by the arrestee, consistent with officer safety and resource availability D. Two officers of different genders conduct the search together

Answer C. Per P.G. 208-05, if the arrestee’s gender is not apparent or the arrestee objects to the assigned gender, the search is conducted by a member of the gender requested by the arrestee, consistent with officer safety and resource availability. Officers must NEVER search a person to determine their gender.

Question 5

Who must authorize a strip search?

A. The desk officer on duty B. The arresting officer’s immediate supervisor or borough court section supervisor C. The commanding officer or duty captain D. Any supervisor of higher rank than the arresting officer

Answer B. Per P.G. 208-05, a strip search requires the knowledge and approval of the arresting officer’s IMMEDIATE SUPERVISOR or the borough court section supervisor. The CO/Duty Captain becomes involved only when there is a disagreement between the desk officer and an outside supervisor.

Question 6

The desk officer and an outside supervisor disagree about whether a strip search should be conducted. Who makes the final decision?

A. The desk officer, as the officer in charge of the command B. The outside supervisor, as the arresting officer’s supervisor C. The CO or Duty Captain, after being notified by the desk officer D. The Legal Bureau, after consultation

Answer C. Per P.G. 208-05, when there is a disagreement between the desk officer and an outside supervisor regarding a strip search, the desk officer notifies the CO/Duty Captain, who makes the FINAL decision.

Question 7

How many parts comprise the Miranda warnings as outlined in the Patrol Guide?

A. Four B. Five C. Six D. Seven

Answer C. Per P.G. 208-09, Miranda warnings consist of SIX parts: (1) right to remain silent, (2) anything said may be used against you, (3) right to attorney before and during questioning, (4) attorney provided if cannot afford one, (5) right to remain silent until consulting attorney, (6) willing to answer questions.

Question 8

A juvenile is arrested and the parent is present. The juvenile agrees to answer questions, but the parent objects. What should the officer do?

A. Proceed with questioning since the juvenile consented B. Cease questioning — the parent’s objection overrides the juvenile’s willingness C. Continue but limit questions to basic identifying information D. Request a Legal Bureau consultation before proceeding

Answer B. Per P.G. 208-03, if a parent or guardian objects to questioning, NO questioning is permitted even if the juvenile is willing to answer. The parent’s objection is controlling.

Question 9

During custodial interrogation, the suspect states I think I should talk to a lawyer. What is the proper response?

A. Continue questioning, as the statement was equivocal B. Ask the suspect to clarify whether they want an attorney C. Cease interrogation immediately D. Advise the suspect of Miranda rights again and continue

Answer C. Per P.G. 208-09, interrogation must cease immediately when a subject indicates they want an attorney or wish to remain silent. Officers should err on the side of cessation when any request for counsel is made.

Question 10

A person is arrested for a Class E felony and presents a valid out-of-state driver’s license. Is the person eligible for a DAT?

A. No, DATs are only for misdemeanors and violations B. No, out-of-state identification is not accepted for DAT processing C. Yes, Class E felonies are DAT-eligible, and out-of-state licenses are a satisfactory form of ID D. Yes, but only if the person also has a local address

Answer C. Per P.G. 208-27, DATs may be issued for Class E felonies, misdemeanors, and violations. Out-of-state driver’s licenses are on the list of satisfactory identification that the Department is REQUIRED to accept.

Question 11

A defendant arrested for a misdemeanor has an investigation card on file. Does this preclude issuance of a DAT?

A. Yes, any prior police contact disqualifies DAT issuance B. Yes, an investigation card indicates the person is known to police C. No, an investigation card does NOT preclude DAT issuance D. No, but the desk officer must obtain supervisor approval

Answer C. Per P.G. 208-27, an investigation card does NOT preclude DAT issuance. Similarly, DNA owed is NOT a disqualifying factor.

Question 12

Which of the following is a DAT disqualifying factor under the Condition of Defendant category?

A. The defendant owes DNA B. The defendant has an investigation card on file C. The defendant failed to appear in court within the last 2 years D. The defendant has a prior misdemeanor conviction

Answer C. Per P.G. 208-81, failure to appear in court within the last 2 YEARS is a disqualifying factor under Category 1 (Condition of Defendant). DNA owed and investigation cards are specifically NOT disqualifying. A prior conviction alone is not a disqualifier.

Question 13

The arresting officer’s next regularly scheduled tour is 5 days away. What must happen regarding DA conferral for a DAT case?

A. The officer confers with the DA upon return in 5 days B. The conferral is assigned to another officer C. The desk officer confers with the DA on the officer’s behalf D. The case is automatically upgraded from DAT to continued custody

Answer B. Per P.G. 208-82, if the arresting officer’s next regularly scheduled tour is more than 4 DAYS away, the DA conferral must be assigned to another officer.

Question 14

Under the Family Court Act, which of the following relationships qualifies as family/household?

A. Current roommates who share an apartment but are not in an intimate relationship B. Former coworkers who dated briefly three years ago C. Persons who have a child in common but were never married D. Business partners who have known each other for ten years

Answer C. Per P.G. 208-36, the FCA definition of family/household includes persons who have a child in common. Casual acquaintances, business relationships, and mere roommates (without a family-type relationship) do not qualify. Note: the NYPD expanded definition would cover persons living together in a family-type relationship, but a simple roommate arrangement is not a family-type relationship.

Question 15

What additional category does the NYPD expanded definition of family/household include beyond the Family Court Act definition?

A. Persons who attend the same school or workplace B. Persons currently or formerly living together in a family-type relationship C. Persons who share a common friend group D. Persons who live in the same apartment building

Answer B. Per P.G. 208-36, the NYPD expands the FCA definition to include persons currently or formerly living together in a family-type relationship. The FCA itself covers: legally married, formerly married, related by marriage, related by blood, child in common, and intimate relationship.

Question 16

A 17-year-old is arrested for misdemeanor harassment against their former spouse. Does concurrent jurisdiction exist between Criminal Court and Family Court?

A. Yes, all three conditions are met B. No, because the offender is under 18 and the charge is not a felony C. No, because harassment is not a family offense D. Yes, because the parties were married

Answer B. Per P.G. 208-36, concurrent jurisdiction requires: (1) family offense, (2) FCA relationship, and (3) offender 18+. A 16-17 year old qualifies ONLY if charged with a felony-level family offense. Misdemeanor harassment by a 17-year-old goes to Criminal Court only.

Question 17

At a domestic violence scene, no certified interpreter is available and the victim speaks limited English. A family member offers to interpret. What should the officer do?

A. Accept the family member as interpreter for the entire investigation B. Decline — only certified interpreters may be used at any DV scene C. Use the family member temporarily to stabilize the scene, then verify information with a certified member or Language Line D. Postpone the investigation until a certified interpreter arrives

Answer C. Per P.G. 208-36, officers should avoid using family members or bystanders as interpreters. However, they may be used temporarily to stabilize the scene. Information must then be verified through a certified member or Language Line.

Question 18

An officer suspects child abuse but decides not to report it because the evidence is inconclusive. What is the legal consequence of willful failure to report suspected child abuse?

A. No consequence if the evidence is inconclusive B. Administrative reprimand only C. Class A misdemeanor plus civil liability D. Class B misdemeanor

Answer C. Per P.G. 208-36, willful failure to report suspected child abuse constitutes a CLASS A MISDEMEANOR and creates civil liability. The standard is suspected abuse — conclusive evidence is not required before reporting.

Question 19

In a lineup, how many total persons must be present?

A. At least 4 (suspect plus 3 others) B. At least 5 (suspect plus 4 others) C. At least 6 (suspect plus 5 others) D. At least 8 (suspect plus 7 others)

Answer C. Per P.G. 208-24, a lineup must include the suspect plus at least 5 OTHER persons, for a minimum of 6 total participants.

Question 20

During lineup preparation, where should the suspect be placed in the lineup?

A. In the center position B. In a position randomly assigned by the detective C. In the position selected by the suspect D. In the last position

Answer C. Per P.G. 208-24, the suspect must be permitted to SELECT their own position in the lineup.

Question 21

The desk officer determines a prisoner is high risk. What is the required escort protocol?

A. One MOS assigned during transport only B. Two MOS assigned during transport to court C. Two MOS assigned to escort the prisoner at ALL times D. One MOS with supervisor check-ins every 30 minutes

Answer C. Per P.G. 208-03, a high risk prisoner requires TWO MOS assigned to escort the prisoner at ALL times — not just during transport, but continuously throughout processing and detention.

Question 22

A person has been arrested three times for Resisting Arrest in the past 4 years. What classification applies?

A. No special classification B. High risk prisoner C. Resisting recidivist — enhance processing with detective squad D. Violent felon — transfer to detective squad for processing

Answer C. Per P.G. 208-03, a Resisting Recidivist is a person arrested 2 or more times within the past 5 YEARS for Resisting Arrest, Assault 2nd (sub 3), Assault on PO, or Aggravated Assault on PO. Three arrests for Resisting Arrest in 4 years qualifies. Processing is enhanced with the detective squad.

Question 23

A foreign national from a mandatory notification country is arrested. The prisoner states they do NOT want their consulate notified. What should the officer do?

A. Honor the prisoner’s wishes and do not notify B. Document the refusal and proceed without notification C. Contact the Intelligence Division Operations Unit — mandatory notification is required regardless of the prisoner’s wishes D. Notify the prisoner’s attorney instead

Answer C. Per P.G. 208-56, for mandatory notification countries, consular notification is REQUIRED regardless of the prisoner’s wishes. The Intelligence Division Operations Unit (available 24 hours) handles mandatory consular notifications.

Question 24

For non-mandatory notification countries, what is the officer’s obligation regarding consular notification?

A. Notify the consulate automatically B. Inform the prisoner of their right to have their embassy/consulate notified C. No obligation — consular notification is optional for non-mandatory countries D. Contact the Intelligence Division for guidance

Answer B. Per P.G. 208-56, for non-mandatory notification countries, the officer must INFORM the prisoner of their right to have their embassy or consulate notified. The prisoner may then choose whether to exercise that right.

Question 25

During DAT processing, the OMNIFORM system shows no NYSID has been returned. How frequently should the officer check for results?

A. Every 15 minutes B. Every 30 minutes maximum C. Every 60 minutes D. Only once, then proceed without NYSID

Answer B. Per P.G. 208-27, if no NYSID is returned during DAT processing, the officer should check back every 30 MINUTES maximum. The prisoner should not be held indefinitely waiting for NYSID results.

Section 209 — Summonses

Source

  • Patrol Guide Section: 209
  • Source PDF: public-pguide1.pdf
  • Priority: Medium
  • Status: Completed

Learning Objectives

  • Understand the conditions under which a summons may be issued instead of an arrest (age, capacity, eligible offenses)
  • Know the VTL and Penal Law exceptions that require arrest rather than summons issuance
  • Identify the correct returnable agency for each summons type (Criminal Court, TVB, OATH, TAB, DOF)
  • Master CJRA civil summons eligibility, OATH Recidivist criteria, and NYPD Codes 1-4
  • Know the 5 mandatory identification elements for parking summonses and the consequences of omission
  • Understand abandoned and derelict vehicle definitions and associated timeframes
  • Recognize the Patrol Supervisor’s responsibilities for Code 3 verification, voided summons review, and summons accountability
  • Know the procedures for suspended/revoked license stops, ignition key removal, and department vehicle summonses
  • Distinguish between Urgent Police Action, Necessary Police Action, and convenience for department vehicle summons defenses
  • Understand TAB return date calculation (28 days) and Criminal Court return date sourcing (FINEST message)

Study Guide Files

File Topics Covered
section-209-conditions-and-types.md Conditions of service, age requirements, VTL/PL exceptions, returnable agencies, CJRA/OATH civil summonses, OATH Recidivist criteria, NYPD Codes 1-4, return dates (209-01, 209-02, 209-03, 209-04)
section-209-parking.md Parking summonses, 5 mandatory ID elements, abandoned/derelict vehicle definitions, 3-summons limit, out-of-state plates, NYSPIN checks, ignition key removal (209-07, 209-08, 209-27)
section-209-traffic.md Personal service requirements, TVB procedures, Criminal Court traffic summonses, TAB summonses, e-bike/e-scooter/bicycle/pedestrian summonses, Vehicle Report requirement, RTR notation (209-09, 209-10, 209-11, 209-15, 209-16, 209-17, 209-18, 209-19)
section-209-oath.md Non-CJRA OATH summonses, vendor/health/noise/sanitation/air/graffiti violations, ECB procedures (209-12, 209-20)
section-209-specialized.md Mandatory license checks, suspended/revoked license procedures, tow truck enforcement, department vehicle summonses, voided summonses, electronic monitoring, red light/speed cameras (209-21, 209-22, 209-23, 209-24, 209-26, 209-28, 209-30, 209-31, 209-32, 209-33, 209-34, 209-35, 209-37, 209-39, 209-40)
key-terms.md All key terms, forms, critical numbers, and abbreviations from Section 209
review-questions.md 15 multiple-choice practice questions with hidden answers

P.G. 209-40 Speed Camera Violations completed

Sergeant Focus Areas

Key Supervisor Procedures

  • P.G. 209-01 — Conditions of Service: Verify officers check age (16+), capacity (able to care for self), and offense eligibility before issuing summonses; ensure VTL exceptions (Leaving Scene, Dangerous Articles, False Statements, Stolen Vehicle, DWI) result in arrest, not summons
  • P.G. 209-03 — CJRA Civil Summons: Patrol supervisor MUST respond to verify Code 3 (law enforcement exception); desk officer makes FINAL determination on identity/residence; verify OATH Recidivist screening before civil summons issuance
  • P.G. 209-07 — Parking Summonses: Monitor 5 mandatory ID elements (plate number, type, expiration, make, model) — omission = dismissal; enforce 3-summons max per vehicle per day; ensure no summonses issued to abandoned/derelict vehicles
  • P.G. 209-09 — Personal Service: Verify Vehicle Reports completed for ALL vehicle/bicycle/e-scooter/e-bike stops; ensure one violation per summons; RTR notation when violator records activity
  • P.G. 209-24 — License Checks: Mandatory at traffic collisions, car stops, and investigatory situations; do NOT detain solely for license check; Activity Log entry when DMV system is inoperative
  • P.G. 209-26 — Suspended License: Verify proper confiscation and PD634-152 preparation; accept 14-day correction proof (validated license, temporary license, DMV receipt); park vehicle legally for registered owner
  • P.G. 209-27 — Ignition Key: 3-minute threshold for removal; PD571-121 in duplicate; 48-hour deadline for Property Clerk invoicing
  • P.G. 209-30 — Dept Vehicle Summons: Review Summons Plea Forms within 30 days; scrutinize necessary police action claims — convenience is NOT a valid defense; know the safety violation list
  • P.G. 209-31 to 209-34 — Voided Summonses: Supervisory review required for all voided summonses; monitor voiding patterns for training deficiencies or integrity concerns

Section 209 — Conditions of Service and Summons Types

Covers P.G. 209-01, 209-02, 209-03, 209-04

P.G. 209-01: Conditions of Service

When a Summons Replaces Arrest

A member of the service is authorized to issue a summons or notice of violation INSTEAD OF making an arrest when the violator meets ALL of the following conditions:

Condition Requirement
Age Violator must be 16 years of age or older
Capacity Violator must be able to care for self (not incapacitated by illness, injury, or intoxication)
Offense type Must fall within an eligible statute (see below)

Eligible Statutes for Summons Issuance

Statute Notes
Agriculture & Markets Law All applicable violations
General Business Law All applicable violations
Labor Law All applicable violations
Multiple Dwelling Law All applicable violations
Vehicle and Traffic Law (VTL) With exceptions — see below
NYC Administrative Code All applicable violations
NYC Health Code All applicable violations
Penal Law violations With exceptions — see below

VTL Exceptions — CANNOT Issue Summons, MUST Arrest

VTL Section Offense
Section 600 Leaving the Scene of an Accident
Section 380 Dangerous Articles (Transportation)
Sections 392 / 392-a False Statements
Sections 421 / 422 / 423 / 426 Stolen Vehicle offenses
Section 1192 DWI / DWAI

Exam Alert: The five VTL exception categories are heavily tested. Remember the mnemonic: L-D-F-S-D (Leaving scene, Dangerous articles, False statements, Stolen vehicle, DWI). These offenses ALWAYS require arrest, never a summons.

Penal Law Violation Exceptions — CANNOT Issue Summons

PL Section Offense
PL 100 Criminal Solicitation
PL 215.58 Failure to Respond to Appearance Ticket
PL 240.40 Public Under Influence of Narcotics or a Drug

16-Year-Olds — Special Rules

Summons Type Eligibility
Criminal Court summonses for ALL violations Yes — eligible
Criminal Court summonses for VTL misdemeanors Yes — eligible
Criminal Court summonses for non-VTL misdemeanors No — not eligible

Sergeant Focus: Supervisors must verify that officers correctly distinguish between violations (summons-eligible for 16-year-olds) and non-VTL misdemeanors (not summons-eligible for 16-year-olds). This is a common error in the field.

P.G. 209-02: Returnable Agencies

Agency Routing Matrix

Offense Type Returnable To
Parking offenses NYC Department of Finance — Parking Violations Operations
Traffic infractions (non-parking) NYS DMV Traffic Violations Bureau (TVB) OR NYC Criminal Court
Pedestrian offenses NYC Criminal Court
Traffic misdemeanors NYC Criminal Court

OATH (Office of Administrative Trials and Hearings) Offenses

Offense Category Returnable To
Food/General Vendor violations OATH
Health Code violations OATH
Public Health Law (Canine Waste) OATH
Noise Code violations OATH
Sanitation violations OATH
Air Code violations OATH
Graffiti violations OATH

Exam Alert: Know the distinction between parking offenses (Dept of Finance) and traffic infractions (TVB or Criminal Court). Pedestrian offenses and traffic misdemeanors always go to Criminal Court — NOT the TVB.

P.G. 209-03: CJRA Civil Summons (OATH)

Criminal Justice Reform Act of 2016

The CJRA allows certain violations to be issued as civil summonses returnable to OATH instead of criminal summonses returnable to Criminal Court.

The 6 CJRA Violations

# Violation Administrative Code Section
1 Open Container (alcohol) AC 10-125(b)
2 Public Urination AC 16-118(6)
3 Littering AC 16-118(1)(a)
4 Spitting AC 16-118(1)(b)
5 Excessive Noise (unreasonable/prohibited) AC 24-218
6 NYC Park Rules Title 56

Exam Alert: Memorize all 6 CJRA violations. A common exam trick is listing a violation NOT on this list (e.g., disorderly conduct) and asking if it qualifies for CJRA civil summons. Also note: CJRA does NOT apply in the transit system — use TAB (Transit Adjudication Bureau) instead.

OATH Recidivist — NOT Eligible for Civil Summons

A violator is classified as an OATH Recidivist and must receive a Criminal Court summons (not OATH civil) if ANY of the following apply:

# Condition
1 2 or more felony arrests in the past 2 years
2 3 or more unanswered OATH summonses in the past 8 years
3 Currently on parole or probation

Exam Alert: The OATH Recidivist criteria are a top exam target. Remember: 2 felonies / 2 years, 3 OATH / 8 years, parole/probation. Any one condition disqualifies the person from receiving a civil summons.

NYPD Codes for CJRA Processing

Code Meaning Notes
Code 1 OATH Recidivist Person meets recidivist criteria — issue Criminal Court summons
Code 2 Active warrant or I-card Person has an active warrant or investigation card on file
Code 3 Law enforcement exception Requires patrol supervisor verification
Code 4 Non-CJRA violation or multiple violations Offense is not one of the 6 CJRA violations, or person committed multiple violations

Sergeant Focus: When a Criminal Court summons is issued under Code 3 (law enforcement exception), a patrol supervisor MUST respond to the scene to verify the exception. This is a mandatory supervisory response — failure to respond is a procedural violation.

Identity and Processing Rules

Situation Action
Violator has no valid ID Remove to command for identification
Violator has active warrant Issue Criminal Court summons; return to issuing court
Identity/residence verification needed Desk officer makes the FINAL determination

P.G. 209-04: Return Date and Time

Return Date Matrix

Summons Type Return Date/Time
Criminal Court Per daily FINEST message
Traffic Violations Bureau (TVB) Per instructions printed on the summons
Transit Adjudication Bureau (TAB) 28 calendar days from date of issuance
Parking Violations No date/time entered by officer — information is pre-printed on the summons

TAB Return Date — Special Rules

  • If the 28th day falls on a weekend or holiday, the return date is the next business day
  • Count begins from the date of issuance, not the date of service

Exam Alert: The 28-day TAB return date is frequently tested. Remember: 28 calendar days, and if it falls on a weekend/holiday, roll forward to the next business day.

Common Exam Angles

  1. VTL exceptions to summons issuance — Expect questions listing offenses and asking which require arrest vs. allow summons
  2. OATH Recidivist criteria — Expect questions presenting a scenario and asking if the person qualifies for CJRA civil summons
  3. NYPD Code 3 — Supervisor verification requirement is a favorite Sergeant exam topic
  4. 16-year-old summons eligibility — Expect questions testing the VTL misdemeanor exception
  5. Returnable agency routing — Parking vs. traffic infraction vs. pedestrian offense routing
  6. TAB 28-day return date — Calculation questions including weekend/holiday adjustments

Sergeant Study Notes

  • The desk officer is the final authority on identity/residence verification for CJRA processing
  • A patrol supervisor must physically respond when Code 3 (law enforcement exception) is used
  • Supervisors should verify that officers check for OATH Recidivist status BEFORE issuing a civil summons
  • The CJRA does NOT apply in the transit system — supervisors must ensure officers use TAB procedures instead
  • When a violator cannot care for themselves (illness, injury, intoxication), a summons is NOT appropriate regardless of the offense — the person must be processed differently

Section 209 — OATH and Environmental Control Board Procedures

Covers P.G. 209-12, 209-20

P.G. 209-12: OATH Summonses (Non-CJRA)

OATH Jurisdiction

The Office of Administrative Trials and Hearings (OATH) handles civil enforcement of various city code violations that do not rise to criminal court level. These are separate from the 6 CJRA violations covered in P.G. 209-03.

Non-CJRA OATH Offenses

Offense Category Examples
Food/General Vendor violations Unlicensed vending, improper vending location
Health Code violations Sanitary code infractions
Public Health Law — Canine Waste Failure to clean up after a dog
Noise Code violations Commercial or construction noise violations
Sanitation violations Improper waste disposal, recycling violations
Air Code violations Emission and air quality infractions
Graffiti violations Possession of graffiti instruments, vandalism

OATH vs. CJRA Distinction

Feature CJRA Civil Summons (P.G. 209-03) Non-CJRA OATH Summons (P.G. 209-12)
Violations 6 specific CJRA violations only Vendor, health, noise, sanitation, air, graffiti
Recidivist check Required (OATH Recidivist criteria) Not applicable
NYPD Codes Codes 1-4 apply Not applicable
Transit system NOT applicable (use TAB) NOT applicable in transit
Returnable to OATH OATH

Exam Alert: Do not confuse CJRA civil summonses with non-CJRA OATH summonses. Both are returnable to OATH, but CJRA summonses require recidivist screening and NYPD Code assignment. Non-CJRA OATH summonses do not.

Processing Requirements

  • Proper identification of the violator
  • Accurate recording of the offense and applicable code section
  • Correct OATH return information
  • Service of summons copy to the violator

P.G. 209-20: Environmental Control Board (ECB) Procedures

Covers enforcement procedures for violations heard by the Environmental Control Board (now part of OATH), including: - Environmental violations within NYC - Building code violations - Zoning violations - Coordination with city inspection agencies - Proper ECB summons completion

Sergeant Focus: Supervisors should ensure officers correctly route OATH-eligible offenses to OATH rather than Criminal Court. Misrouting creates unnecessary court appearances and administrative burden.

Common Exam Angles

  1. CJRA vs. non-CJRA OATH — Both go to OATH, but CJRA requires recidivist screening
  2. OATH offense categories — Know which violations are OATH-returnable
  3. Routing errors — Questions may present a scenario where an officer incorrectly routes an OATH violation to Criminal Court
  4. Transit system exclusion — OATH summonses (both CJRA and non-CJRA) do NOT apply in the transit system

Sergeant Study Notes

  • Train officers on the distinction between CJRA and non-CJRA OATH violations
